title: East Riding of Yorkshire Council
text:
Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: East Riding of Yorkshire Council 
Address: County Hall 
Cross Street 
Beverley 
East Yorkshire 
HU17 9BA 
  
 
 
Decision (including any steps ordered) 
1. The complainant has requested information about the total amount 
collected by Bridlington Park and Ride parking machines during 5-12 
April 2025. East Riding of Yorkshire Council (the council) refused to 
disclose the requested information citing section 31(1)(a) of FOIA (law 
enforcement) as its basis for doing so. 
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that the council has correctly applied 
section 31(1)(a) of FOIA to withhold the requested information and that 
the public interest favours maintaining the exemption. 
3. The Commissioner does not require further steps, as a result of this 
decision notice. 
 
 
 
 
 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 2 
Request and response 
4. On 16 April 2025, the complainant wrote to the council and requested 
information in the following terms: 
“I request you accept an FOI: The amount taken by the parking 
machines in Bridlington Park & Ride for the period 5-12th April this 
year.” 
5. On 14 May 2025, the council provided its response, in which it confirmed 
that it held the requested information but that it was being withheld 
under section 31 of FOIA. 
6. Upon receiving this response, the complainant asked the council to 
conduct an internal review, on 14 May 2025. On 16 May 2025, the 
council provided its internal review in which it maintained its original 
position. 
Scope of the case 
7. The complainant contacted the Commissioner on 12 June 2025 to 
complain about the way their request for information had been handled. 
8. During his investigation, the Commissioner queried which part of section 
31 the council was relying on to withhold the requested information. The 
council confirmed that it was relying upon section 31(1)(a). 
9. Therefore, the Commissioner considers that the scope of his 
investigation is to consider the application of section 31(1)(a) of FOIA to 
the requested information. 
Reasons for decision 
Section 31 - law enforcement 
10. Section 31(1)(a) of FOIA allows a public authority to withhold 
information if its disclosure under the Act would, or would be likely to, 
prejudice the prevention or detection of crime. 
11. In order for section 31 to be engaged, the following criteria must be 
met: 
 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 3 
• the actual harm which the public authority claims would, or would be 
likely to occur if the withheld information was disclosed has to relate 
to the applicable interests within the relevant exemptions (in this 
case, the prevention or detection of crime); 
• the public authority must be able to demonstrate that some causal 
relationship exists between the potential disclosure of the information 
being withheld and the prejudice which the exemption is designed to 
protect. Furthermore, the resultant prejudice, which is alleged must 
be real, actual or of substance; and, 
• it is necessary to establish whether the level of likelihood of prejudice 
being relied upon by the public authority is met – i.e. disclosure 
‘would be likely’ to result in prejudice or disclosure ‘would’ result in 
prejudice. 
12. In his published guidance, the Commissioner recognises that section 
31(1)(a) will cover all aspects of the prevention and detection of crime. 
13. Consideration of the exemption at section 31 is a two-stage process; 
even if the exemption is engaged, the information must be disclosed 
unless the public interest in maintaining the exemption outweighs the 
public interest in disclosure. 
The applicable interest 
14. The first step in considering whether this exemption is engaged is to 
consider whether the actual harm claimed   is relevant to ‘the prevention 
or detection of crime’ as mentioned in section 31(1)(a). 
15. In submissions to the Commissioner, the council explained that 
disclosure of the requested information could “provide operational 
patterns of high periods of cash collections in relation to parking fares or 
identify potential vulnerabilities in our payment methods that could be 
exploited.” This would, the council explained, make it more vulnerable to 
crime. 
16. The Commissioner is satisfied that the prejudice the council is 
envisaging in this case, is relevant to the particular interests which 
section 31(1)(a) is designed to protect. 
The nature of the prejudice 
17. The Commissioner next considered whether the council have 
demonstrated a causal link between the disclosure of the withheld 
information and the prejudice that section 31(1)(a) is designed to 
protect. In his view, disclosure must be at least capable of harming the 
interest in some way, ie, have a damaging or detrimental effect on it. 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 4 
18. In submissions to the Commissioner, the council explained that the 
complainant had requested information relating to a “specific period of 
time…and a specific location”, and that the site uses a range of payment 
methods, including cash, card and permit schemes. 
19. The council further explained that if the information was to be disclosed 
it would “allow individuals to identify patterns or trends” within its 
income streams at specific car park locations. 
20. It added that such patterns may differ from what might normally be 
expected at certain times of the year, for example during bank holidays 
or school holidays. 
21. In evidencing this causal link, the council referred to “widely published 
news articles on the rise of parking scams linked to fake QR codes being 
placed on parking machines.” It argued that disclosing income figures 
for a limited timeframe would “open the council up to being targeted on 
similar dates/time periods.” 
Likelihood of prejudice 
22. The council in its initial response and internal review response used the 
word “could.” 
23. The Commissioner has therefore interpreted this to mean that the 
prejudice, as described above, ‘would be likely’ to occur if the withheld 
information were to be disclosed. The Commissioner accepts that this is 
a credible level of likelihood. 
Is the exemption engaged? 
24. The Commissioner notes that the council did not provide any news 
articles to evidence “the rise of parking scams linked to fake QR codes 
being placed on parking machines.” 
25. However, through his own brief online enquiries, the Commissioner 
identified a range of national and local articles which have reported on 
this issue. 
26. The Commissioner notes the complainant’s concerns that they have not 
asked for “how and when the machines are emptied or cashed up”. 
27. However, the Commissioner considers that the risk extends beyond the 
routine emptying of the machines. Disclosure of the withheld 
information could provide intelligence to individuals with criminal intent 
regarding peak usage times. This could then make the council 
vulnerable to offences such as credit-card or QR scams, as well as 
targeted vandalism of the machines to access cash payments. 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 5 
28. He also considers that as the request identifies a specific named location 
and a very limited time period, the risk arising from disclosure is real. 
He is therefore satisfied that the prejudice being claimed is “real, actual 
or of substance”, and that there is a causal link between disclosure and 
the prejudice claimed. 
29. In addition, the exemption does not require the council to demonstrate 
that it is holding the information for the purpose of preventing and 
detecting crime, it only has to demonstrate that disclosing the 
information could harm efforts to prevent or detect it. 
30. Having considered the circumstances of this case, the Commissioner has 
therefore decided that section 31(1)(a) of FOIA is engaged. He has gone 
on to consider the public interest test. 
Public interest test 
The public interest in disclosing the information 
31. The council recognised that it has a duty to be transparent, and that it is 
“open and honest” in proactive publication of this type of information. 
32.  The complainant argued that the basis of their request was to discover 
the “total funds taken from the General Public without authority.” 
The public interest in maintaining the exemption 
33. The council explained that it already publishes this information Civil 
parking enforcement annual statistics and annual parking report on its 
website, and it provided the link to this in its initial response. However, 
it acknowledged that the published information is not at the granular 
level of detail requested. 
34. The council argued that it has a “duty to protect the public from 
potential fraud and loss of public money.” It stated that it is in the public 
interest to safeguard its revenues streams, such as those generated by 
car parking machines, from either an increased risk of vandalism and 
theft, including the theft of cash, as well as fraudulent methods 
involving credit card misuse or QR code scams. 
35. Any criminal activity, the council argued, would result in public expense 
to deal with the crime and loss of income. 
The balance of the public interest 
36. The Commissioner accepts that there is always a public interest in public 
authorities being open and transparent. 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 6 
37. The Commissioner notes that this request stems from a complaint, 
submitted to the council, regarding alleged “misleading signs and a 
demand for payment.”  
38. However, the Commissioner considers that the requested information is 
of limited public interest. Its relevance is confined largely to the 
complainant and any other individual who may have parked in that 
carpark during the specific period of time. 
39. The Commissioner notes the complainant’s comment that “observations 
revealed many 100s” of payments being made. However, he considers 
that if an individual believes they have incurred an improper charge or 
fine, there are established and accessible routes for challenging such 
matters directly with the council, as the requester has already done. 
40. The Commissioner does not consider that the disclosure of the 
requested information would resolve or help with any person’s concerns 
regarding an unnecessary payment/and or potential fine. 
41. Instead, he considers that disclosure could make the council more 
vulnerable to acts of criminal activity through revealing financial details 
that could be used to facilitate fraud or other unlawful conduct. 
42. For these reasons, the Commissioner is satisfied that in all the 
circumstances of the case, the balance of the public interest is weighted 
in favour of maintaining the exemption.  Therefore, the council was 
entitled to rely on sections 31(1)(a) of FOIA to withhold the requested 
information. 

Reference:  IC-393971-B4K5 
 
 7 
Right of appeal  
 
43. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:  
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights) 
General Regulatory Chamber  
PO Box 11230 
Leicester  
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
44. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website. 
45. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent. 
 
 
 
Victoria McGillicuddy 
Senior Case Officer 
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF

title: Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea
text:
Reference: IC-471148-H6H9 
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
 
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: Royal Borough of Kensington & Chelsea 
Address: The Town Hall 
Hornton Street 
London 
W8 7NX 
 
1. Request: 
The complainant requested the following information from Royal Borough 
of Kensington & Chelsea on 27 October 2025: 
 
“Requested Information 
1. Any detail(s) that is (or are) concerned with the available bathing or 
showering (or both) facility(ies) in the bathroom that is sited in Flat 02 
Apollo House, Cremorne Estate, London, SW10 0DW.  
Note 
This request for information to The RBKC specifically relates to whether the 
property that is referred to in paragraph 1 above had a fully functioning 
level access shower installed or fitted inside the premises on the 17th 
October 2025. 
Section 16(1) Freedom of Information Act 2000 
I rely upon section 16(1) FOlA 2000 (to any extent that may be 
necessary).” 
2. Commissioner’s Decision:  

Reference: IC-471148-H6H9 
 
 2 
A public authority will breach section 10 of FOIA if it fails to respond to a 
request within 20 working days. Based on evidence available to the 
Commissioner, by the date of this notice the public authority has not issued 
a substantive response to this request. Therefore, the Commissioner finds 
a breach of section 10. 
3. Steps: 
The public authority must provide a substantive response to the request in 
accordance with its obligations under FOIA.   
The public authority must take this step within 30 calendar days of the 
date of this decision notice. Failure to comply may result in the 
Commissioner making written certification of this fact to the High Court 
pursuant to section 54 of FOIA and may be dealt with as a contempt of 
court. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
4. Right of Appeal: 

Reference: IC-471148-H6H9 
 
 3 
Information Commissioner 
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF  
 
Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the First-
tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals process 
may be obtained from: 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights) 
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-chamber  
Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 (calendar) 
days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.
description: The complainant has requested information about the available bathing or showering facility in the bathroom in Apollo House, Cremorne Estate, London. The Commissioner’s decision is that the Royal Borough has failed to respond to the request within 20 working days and has therefore breached Section 10 of the Freedom of Information Act. The Commissioner requires the public authority to take the following steps to ensure compliance with the legislation: issue a substantive response, under the FOIA, to the request.

title: Cabinet Office
text:
Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: Cabinet Office 
Address: 70 Whitehall 
London 
SW1A 2AS 
  
 
 
Decision (including any steps ordered) 
1. The complainant submitted a request to the Cabinet Office seeking all 
National Security Council records, minutes, readouts and handwritten 
notes relating to the potential proscription of Palestine Action. The 
Cabinet Office refused to confirm or deny whether it held the requested 
information on the basis of section 35(3) of FOIA by virtue of section 
35(1)(b) (Ministerial communications). 
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that the Cabinet Office is entitled to rely 
on section 35(3) of FOIA to refuse to confirm or deny whether it holds 
the requested information. 
3. The Commissioner does not require further steps. 
Background 
4. The National Security Council (NSC) is the main forum for collective 
discussion of the government’s strategic approach to national security, 
foreign policy, resilience, international relations, economic security, 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 2 
trade, development, defence and global issues. It is chaired by the 
Prime Minister and is a Cabinet committee.1 
5. Palestine Action was proscribed under the Terrorism Act 2000 (TA 2000) 
on 5 July 2025. 
6. Palestine Action sought to challenge this decision2 and on 13 February 
2026 the High Court found the decision to proscribe Palestine Action was 
unlawful.3 
Request and response 
7. The complainant submitted the following request to the Cabinet Office 
on 10 July 2025: 
“Please provide  
 
1. All National Security Council (NSC) records, minutes, readouts, 
handwritten notes relating to the potential proscription of Palestine 
Action (2020-2025).” 
 
8. The Cabinet Office responded on 11 August 2025 and explained that:   
“We are writing to advise you that The Cabinet Office neither confirms 
nor denies that it holds information you have requested, under section 
35(1) of the Freedom of Information Act. The duty in section 1(1)(a) of 
the Act does not apply, by virtue of section 35(1)(a) of the Act.”   
 
9. The response went on to explain that the balance of the public interest 
test favoured maintaining the neither confirm nor deny (NCND) position. 
10. The complainant contacted the Cabinet Office on the same day and 
asked it to conduct an internal review of this refusal.  
11. The Cabinet Office informed him of the outcome of the internal review 
on 17 September 2025. This explained that it was satisfied that the 
exemption contained at section 35(3) of FOIA was engaged on the 
following basis: 
 
 
1 https://www.gov.uk/government/groups/national-security-council  
2 https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cqlerg1n67no  
3 https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c3wleezq73no  

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 3 
“The information you have requested, if held (which is neither 
confirmed nor denied) would relate to Ministerial communications on 
the basis that you have sought information that concerns the NSC, 
which Ministers attend. It follows that the Cabinet Office neither 
confirms nor denies whether it holds information under section 35(3) of 
the Act.” 
 
12. The internal review response went on to explain that the Cabinet Office 
was satisfied that the public interest favoured maintaining the exclusion 
to confirm or deny whether information was held. 
Scope of the case 
13. The complainant contacted the Commissioner on 17 September 2025 in 
order to complain about the Cabinet Office’s refusal of his request. 
14. During the course of the Commissioner’s investigation the Cabinet Office 
explained that it was also relying sections 23(5) (security bodies), 24(2) 
(national security) and 31(3) to refuse to confirm or deny whether it 
held any information falling within the scope of the request. 
15. It is important to recognise that the Commissioner’s role in considering 
the application of exemptions is limited to considering the circumstances 
at the time the request should have been responded to in accordance 
with the statutory timeframes for compliance set out in FOIA. That is, at 
the 20 working days limit and not the circumstances that exist at the 
point that the decision notice is being issued. 
16. Therefore whilst there have been developments in respect of the 
decision to proscribe Palestine Action as described in the Background 
section above, since these post-date 7 August 2025 (ie the twentieth 
working day after the request was submitted) these are not relevant to 
the Commissioner’s decision in respect of whether the exemptions have 
been applied correctly. 
Reasons for decision 
Section 35(3) 
17. Section 35(3) states that: 
“The duty to confirm or deny does not arise in relation to information 
which is (or if it were held by the public authority would be) exempt 
information by virtue of subsection (1).”  

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 4 
18. The practical consequence of this is that if the requested information (if 
held) fell within the scope of any of the exemptions contained in section 
35(1) of FOIA then the Cabinet Office can refuse to confirm or deny 
whether it holds it, subject to a balance of public interest test. 
19. The Cabinet Office explained that the relevant exemption was section 
35(1)(b) which provides an exemption for information which relates to 
Ministerial communications. 
20. In support of this position the Cabinet Office explained that the 
information (if held) would constitute ministerial communications by 
virtue of section 35(5) of FOIA, which notes that ‘Ministerial 
communications’ includes the proceedings of the Cabinet or of any 
committee of the Cabinet, which would include the NSC. 
21. The Commissioner is satisfied that the requested information, if held, 
would relate to proceedings of the NSC, a Cabinet committee and 
therefore, if held, would fall within the exemption provided by section 
35(1)(b) as such information would relate to a form of ministerial 
communication. The Commissioner is therefore satisfied that section 
35(3) is engaged. 
Public interest test 
22. Section 35 is a qualified exemption and therefore the Commissioner 
must consider the public interest test and whether in all the 
circumstances of the case the public interest in maintaining the 
exemption outweighs the public interest in confirming whether or not 
the requested information is held. 
Public interest in maintaining the exemption 
23. The Cabinet Office provided the Commissioner with detailed 
submissions, set out under a number of different sub-headings, to 
support its position that the public interest favoured maintaining the 
exemption. The Commissioner has replicated these submissions below. 
The principle of Cabinet Collective Responsibility 
24. The Cabinet Office explained that the timing, attendance, agenda items 
and contents of Cabinet committee meetings are not normally put into 
the public domain with the Cabinet Office Manual noting at paragraph 
11.18 that:  
“the Government’s working assumption is that information relating to 
the proceedings of Cabinet and its committees should remain 
confidential.” 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 5 
25. The Cabinet Office argued that confirming whether or not it held the 
requested information would undermine the principle of Cabinet 
collective responsibility. 
26. It explained that the convention of Cabinet collective responsibility 
exists to underpin consistent and coherent government and has been a 
foundational pillar of the UK’s system of Cabinet government and the 
UK’s constitutional framework since the eighteenth century. The Cabinet 
Office argued that Parliament explicitly recognised the public interest in 
the maintenance of Cabinet collective responsibility, for example via 
section 36(2)(a)(i) and the provisions of section 35(3) of FOIA. 
27. The Cabinet Office explained that the principle is referred to in the 
Ministerial Code: 
“The principle of collective responsibility requires that ministers should 
be able to express their views frankly in the expectation that they can 
argue freely in private while maintaining a united front when decisions 
have been reached. This in turn requires that the privacy of opinions 
expressed in cabinet and ministerial committees, including in 
correspondence, should be maintained.” 
28. And: 
“The internal process through which a decision has been made, or the 
level of Committee by which it was taken should not be disclosed. 
Neither should the individual views of Ministers or advice provided by 
civil servants as part of that internal process be disclosed. Decisions 
reached by the Cabinet or Ministerial Committees are binding on all 
members of the Government.” 
29. The Cabinet Office explained that the same principles applied to the 
committees of the Cabinet with the Cabinet Manual noting that the 
Cabinet committee structure: 
“supports the principle of collective responsibility, ensuring that policy 
proposals receive thorough consideration without an issue having to be 
referred to the whole Cabinet.” 
30. The Cabinet Office argued that it was necessary to NCND whether it held 
the requested information because either response would amount to the 
disclosure of an agenda item of a Cabinet committee. The Cabinet Office 
argued that the logical conclusion of such a response would be to 
regularly disclose the business of Cabinet and its committees. In its view 
such disclosures would be prejudicial to the convention of collective 
responsibility in the following ways: 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 6 
• Disclosure would lead to a reduction in the candour of ministerial 
discussions in these fora as disclosure would contribute to public 
debate and speculation about the contributions of individual ministers 
to the meeting;  
• The routine disclosure of what subjects had been discussed by Cabinet 
committees would adversely affect the ability of ministers to organise 
themselves as they see fit to discharge the collective business of 
government in the most effective and efficient way, without regard to 
public commentary on the way in which collective agreement was 
sought, or speculation about the substantive content of ministerial 
discussions in these fora; and  
• The routine disclosure of Cabinet (and Cabinet committee) meeting 
agendas would undermine the operation of the convention of Cabinet 
collective responsibility. 
31. In the circumstances of this case the Cabinet Office argued that if it 
were to confirm or deny whether it held the requested information it 
would make it apparent whether or not the NSC had discussed the 
subject of Palestine Action. As noted above, in its view the logic of 
issuing such a response is that it would be possible, through a series of 
requests, to establish what the agenda of the NSC is on a regular basis, 
together with the agendas of the Cabinet and other Cabinet committees. 
The quality of discussions at Cabinet and its committees 
32. The Cabinet Office emphasised, again as noted above, that the agendas 
of Cabinet committees are not normally disclosed in order to protect the 
safe and private space for ministerial discussion in these fora. In its view 
confirmation that a Cabinet committee had (or had not) discussed a 
particular topic would undermine the quality of discussions in these 
settings. 
33. The Cabinet Office explained that aside from full Cabinet, Cabinet 
committees are the highest decision making bodies of the government. 
As a result, such meetings are subject to significant public interest, 
particularly on high profiles matters such as the proscription of Palestine 
Action. The Cabinet Office argued that disclosure of the agenda items 
would result in public speculation about the contributions of individuals 
at those meeting. In its view the public may make approximations about 
the contributions of individual ministers based on their public profiles as 
a politician, or their previous public pronouncements on these issues. As 
a result, the Cabinet Office argued that ministers may therefore feel 
incentivised to give contributions based on how they will be received by 
audiences outside of the room, beyond the requirements of frank, 
candid and effective discussion of all policy options. As a result, the 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 7 
quality of discussions at Cabinet committees would decline, leading to 
worse decision making outcomes. 
The Cabinet committee process 
34. The Cabinet Office also argued that the NCND response was necessary 
to protect the sovereignty of the collective deliberative process of 
Cabinet committees such as the NSC. The Cabinet Office explained that 
Cabinet committees help to ensure that government business is 
processed more effectively relieving pressure on Cabinet. The committee 
structure also supports the principle of Cabinet collective responsibility, 
ensuring that policy proposals receive thorough consideration without an 
issue having to be referred to the whole Cabinet. The Cabinet Office 
argued that ministers should be able to organise themselves as they see 
fit in order to order to discharge the collective business of government 
in the absence of premature public scrutiny. The Cabinet Office argued 
that revealing which subjects were disclosed at which meetings would 
result in ministers being incentivised to either increase or decrease the 
frequency of meetings, meet to discuss a particular issue to be seen to 
do so or choose not to meet to discuss certain controversial issues for 
risk of undue scrutiny. Consequently, agendas may become less candid 
and the efficiency and candour of discussions would decrease. 
35. The Cabinet Office added that ministers may seek to escalate matters to 
the Cabinet or a Cabinet committee in anticipation of being seen to raise 
them at either of these fora. Conversely, ministers may be mindful 
about bringing highly contentious subjects to a Cabinet committee 
because they would be mindful of the later public reaction when the fact 
of that subject being discussed becomes known. Furthermore, the 
Cabinet committee agendas are by their nature brief, which may in turn 
generate public speculation about what exactly the government’s plans 
are. 
36. The Cabinet Office also highlighted a scenario if a particular matter was 
acknowledged to have been discussed at multiple Cabinet committee 
meetings without it being apparent that a decision had been made, it 
would encourage unhelpful speculation that the Cabinet was too divided 
on the subject, that the Cabinet was incapable of making a decision over 
it or that the subject was more intractable than was actually the case. 
Operation of the convention of Cabinet Collective Responsibility in public 
37. A regular disclosure of items discussed at Cabinet (and Cabinet 
committees) could prematurely reveal what progress the government is 
(or is not, as they case may be) making in respect of the development 
of a certain policy. Inferences may be drawn if it were to be repeatedly 
confirmed that the Cabinet had not discussed a certain issue that had 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 8 
salience among the general public. It may also lead to conclusions being 
reached as to which subjects were being prioritised at Cabinet level, 
thereby encroaching on the ability of ministers to order their business as 
they see fit. 
38. As a result, a sufficient number of disclosures as to what subjects 
constituted the business of the Cabinet would effectively expose certain 
dynamics of the Cabinet. In such circumstances, it would be increasingly 
difficult to maintain Cabinet collective responsibility, which depends on 
there being a unified public position, which should not be undermined by 
disclosures which would encourage speculation as to the extent to which 
the Cabinet did (or did not) agree on certain subjects. 
The responsibility of departments 
39. The Cabinet Office argued that complying with section 1(1)(a) of FOIA 
would interfere with the responsibility of the government to inform the 
public as to how it is performing its functions and the decisions that it 
has made. 
40. Paragraph 4.43 of the Cabinet Manual notes that:  
“Ministers are responsible for ensuring that their departments take 
whatever action is necessary to implement decisions made by Cabinet 
or Cabinet committees, and for reporting back to colleagues on 
progress if needed.” 
41. The Cabinet Office explained that it was the responsibility of ministers 
and departments to carry out the decisions of Cabinet through 
legislation or otherwise and to advise the public the actions they taking 
following decisions made at Cabinet level. Confirmation or denial as to 
the subjects at Cabinet or Cabinet committees would effectively short 
cut that process. That is to say, the public would be aware of topics 
discussed at Cabinet before departments had established how decisions 
of Cabinet should be implemented or presented.   
42. The Cabinet Office argued that this would lead to considerable disruption 
or confusion. It highlighted that discussion of a topic may suggest to the 
public that the government had reached a decision on this, but that is 
not always the case. It is common practice for Cabinet committee 
meetings to decide to send matters back to departments for further 
work, or to escalate a matter to a different Cabinet committee meeting 
(attended by more senior ministers) or to the Cabinet itself. 
43. In certain circumstances, government departments also could feel under 
pressure to issue announcements before the fact of a certain subject 
having been discussed at Cabinet is disclosed. Confidence in a 
government department may be undermined if an announcement on a 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 9 
particular matter was expected while it had, in fact, been sent back to 
the Cabinet (or Cabinet committee) for a further decision. In such 
circumstances, the management of public expectations in respect of 
particular policy areas could become very difficult. 
44. The Cabinet Office highlighted that minutes are part of the historic 
record of government and are handled in line with the Public Records 
Act 1958. If the government considered it beneficial to do so it would 
regularly publish the agenda items of Cabinet meetings. However, it did 
not do so and instead there is a 20 year period before such minutes to 
The National Archives so they can form part of the public record. 
Public interest in confirming or denying whether the information is held 
45. The Cabinet Office acknowledged that there is a public interest in there 
being greater transparency around the matter of how government is 
conducted at the highest level and in understanding what the Cabinet 
(and Cabinet committees) discusses. 
46. The complainant argued that the public interest outweighed the public 
interest in maintaining the exemption because the requested 
information related to a decision by Cabinet ministers to proscribe what 
was previously defined as a protest group. 
47. The complainant argued that this decision caused unprecedented impact 
on ECHR article 10 and 11 human rights and fundamental freedoms of 
the entire population of the UK. He argued that it was of vital public 
interest that the public have access to information on how this decision 
was made, who supported it, and on what grounds it was made. In his 
view this required confirmation or denial of NSC involvement, and if 
confirmed, full disclosure of all related records. 
48. The complainant argued that it is already in the public domain that the 
Home Secretary made the decision to proscribe Palestine Action after 
consulting other government departments such as the Foreign, 
Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) and Department for 
Business and Trade (DBT), as well as the security services. He 
suggested that it would therefore not be particularly revealing to 
discover the issue was discussed by the NSC (if indeed that were the 
case). 
49. The complainant also noted that that there was a long history of the 
government of the day raising protest issues in Cabinet committee 
meetings, such as the MISC 13 Cabinet Committee on Animal Rights 
Activists 2001-2006 (see footnote 5 below). 
  

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 10 
Balance of the public interest arguments 
50. The Commissioner accepts that it is for the Cabinet and the government 
to determine the level at which matters are discussed and that process, 
in itself, deserves protection. Furthermore, the Commissioner also 
accepts that if confirmation or denial of the level at which decisions are 
made were routinely provided, this would inevitably lead to increased 
pressure on the government over process issues in terms of the 
organisation of Cabinet and Cabinet committee business and that this 
would be contrary to the public interest for the reasons identified by the 
Cabinet Office. In addition, the Commissioner accepts that there is a 
significant public interest in maintaining and respecting the principle of 
collective Cabinet responsibility. Confirmation as to whether or not the 
requested information is held would undermine that principle by 
revealing whether ministers had discussed a particular issue and within 
a particular forum. As a result the Commissioner accepts that there is 
significant public interest in maintaining the exemption. The 
Commissioner notes that accepting that such weight applies to these 
arguments in this case is in line with his findings in other cases in 
involving requests which sought to establish whether certain topics had 
been discussed at Cabinet or Cabinet Committees.4 
51. With regard to the public interest in favour of confirming or denying 
whether the Cabinet Office holds any information, the Commissioner 
acknowledges that doing so would add, to some degree, to the public’s 
understanding of the decision making process regarding the proscribing 
of Palestine Action. Furthermore, the Commissioner recognises this 
decision has attracted considerable public attention, and along with 
criticism, and at the point that the request was submitted was the 
subject of legal challenge. He accepts that such factors add weight to 
the public interest in confirming whether or not this matter was 
discussed at the NSC in order to potentially provide some transparency 
and clarity as to the forum and level within government at which this 
decision may have been reached.  
52. However, in the Commissioner’s view there is a limit to how informative 
such a confirmation or denial would be. As the Cabinet Office has set 
out, regardless as to the level at which decisions may have been taken – 
once a decision has been made this then becomes the government’s 
position which ministers, in line with the convention of Cabinet collective 
responsibility, then support. Moreover, as the Cabinet Office noted it is 
the role of the relevant department – in this case the Home Office – to 
 
 
4 FS50601833, FS50535179, IC-76534-Q2X7 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 11 
translate any such decision which is taken into action. Therefore, whilst 
compliance with section 1(1)(a) would arguably make the government’s 
decision making process more transparent, beyond the high profile 
nature of the proscription, the Commissioner is not clear why confirming 
whether or not this decision was discussed specifically at NSC is so 
pressing. This is because ultimately this decision was the government’s 
decision, not simply the decision of one of its Cabinet committees which 
may (or may) have discussed the matter. 
53. The Commissioner notes that the complainant argued that it is in the 
public domain that the Home Secretary decided to proscribe Palestine 
Action after consulting other government departments such as the FCDO 
and DBT and also after consulting the security services. The 
Commissioner has not been provided with any sources which pre-date 
the request (or are dated up to 7 August 2025) to support this point. 
However, even if this were the case the Commissioner considers that 
any confirmation that the Home Secretary discussed the matter with 
such bodies to be materially different from a confirmation that the 
matter may have been discussed at the NSC, not least because any such 
engagement with such bodies could have taken place outside of the 
NSC.  
54. With regard to the complainant’s reference to the MISC13 Cabinet 
Committee, the Commissioner notes that this was a entirely different 
committee, the Ministerial Committee on Animal Rights Activists (and 
subsequently Ministerial Committee on Animal Rights Extremism).5 He 
does not consider that simply because the remit of such a committee – 
who’s role as the name suggests - was to consider protest groups, that 
this equates to an expectation that 20 years later the NSC would also be 
a forum within which specific protest groups are likely to be discussed.  
55. Taking the above into account, whilst the Commissioner accepts that 
confirming whether or not the NSC discussed this issue would contribute 
to transparency in respect of this, and whilst he acknowledges the 
controversial nature of the decision, in the Commissioner’s view such 
interests are significantly outweighed by the public interest in 
maintaining the exemption given the impact this would have on the 
government’s decision making processes more broadly. Consequently, 
the Commissioner has found that the public interest favours maintaining 
the exemption contained at section 35(3). 
 
 
5 https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/2006-06-
29/debates/06062967000024/MinisterialCommitteesOfTheCabinet(MISC13)  

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 12 
56. In view of this decision the Commissioner has not considered the 
Cabinet Office’s application of the other exemptions it cited to refuse this 
request. 

Reference:  IC-427919-Y6C6 
 
 13 
Right of appeal  
57. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:  
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights)   
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
58. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website.  
59. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.  
 
 
 
 
Jonathan Slee 
Senior Case Officer 
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF
description: The complainant submitted a request to the Cabinet Office seeking all National Security Council records, minutes, readouts and handwritten notes relating to the potential proscription of Palestine Action. The Cabinet Office refused to confirm or deny whether it held the requested information on the basis of section 35(3) of FOIA by virtue of section 35(1)(b) (Ministerial communications). The Commissioner’s decision is that the Cabinet Office is entitled to rely on section 35(3) of FOIA to refuse to confirm or deny whether it holds the requested information.

title: The Clerk of The House - House of Commons
text:
Reference:  IC-398269-P5K6 
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: House of Commons 
Address: Houses of Parliament  
St Margaret Street  
London  
SW1A 0AA 
 
Decision (including any steps ordered) 
1. The complainant has requested information about role upgrades. The 
House of Commons (the HoC) relied on section 40(2) of FOIA (third 
party personal information) to withhold the information. 
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that the HoC has correctly relied on 
section 40(2) of FOIA to withhold the information. 
3. The Commissioner does not require further steps to be taken. 
Request and response 
4. On 19 March 2025, the complainant wrote to the public authority and 
requested information in the following terms: 
“Under the Freedom of Information Act 2000, I would like to request 
the following information:  
1. A summary of all role upgrades that have been agreed in the last 
two financial years (up to 19 March 2025) within:  
a) PAS ( People and Asset Strategy) - including any previous names 
this directorate may have had during this period  
b) Strategic Estates - including any previous names this department 
may have had during this period  

Reference:  IC-398269-P5K6 
 
 2 
2. For each upgrade, please specify:  
• The method of assessment used, including but not limited to Job 
Evaluation and Grading Support (JEGS) assessments, pay band 
assessments, or any other evaluation methods employed.  
• The specific policies and sections of those policies that were used 
to justify the upgrades.” 
5. The HoC responded on 15 April 2025. It stated that it was withholding 
the requested information under section 40(2). This position was upheld 
during its internal review.  
Scope of the case 
6. The complainant contacted the Commissioner on 26 June 2025 to 
complain about the way their request for information had been handled.  
7. The Commissioner considers that the scope of his investigation is to 
establish whether the HoC is entitled to withhold the requested 
information under section 40(2) of FOIA. 
Reasons for decision 
Section 40 - personal information 
8. Section 40(2) of FOIA provides that information is exempt from 
disclosure if it is the personal data of an individual other than the 
requester and where one of the conditions listed in section 40(3A)(3B) 
or 40(4A) is satisfied. 
9. In this case the relevant condition is contained in section 40(3A)(a). This 
applies where the disclosure of the information to any member of the 
public would contravene any of the principles relating to the processing 
of personal data (‘the DP principles’), as set out in Article 5 of the UK 
General Data Protection Regulation (‘UK GDPR’). 
10. The first step for the Commissioner is to determine whether the withheld 
information constitutes personal data as defined by the Data Protection 
Act 2018 (‘DPA’). If it is not personal data then section 40 of FOIA 
cannot apply. 
11. Secondly, and only if the Commissioner is satisfied that the requested 
information is personal data, he must establish whether disclosure of 
that data would breach any of the DP principles. 

Reference:  IC-398269-P5K6 
 
 3 
Is the information personal data? 
12. Section 3(2) of the DPA defines personal data as: 
“any information relating to an identified or identifiable living individual”. 
13. The two main elements of personal data are that the information must 
relate to a living person and that the person must be identifiable.  
14. An identifiable living individual is one who can be identified, directly or 
indirectly, in particular by reference to an identifier such as a name, an 
identification number, location data, an online identifier or to one or 
more factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, 
economic, cultural or social identity of the individual. 
15. Information will relate to a person if it is about them, linked to them, 
has biographical significance for them, is used to inform decisions 
affecting them or has them as its main focus. 
16. In the circumstances of this case, having considered the withheld 
information, the Commissioner is satisfied that the information does 
relate to the data subject(s). This is because the request is seeking 
information about role upgrades within two specific departments. It then 
goes on to ask for the method of assessment used in any of the role 
upgrades and specific policies used when justifying any upgrades.  
17. Information which relates to the data subject(s)’ job, whether they have 
successfully received a role upgrade, and what specific assessment and 
policy was used to approve the role upgrade would clearly relate to the 
individual(s).   
18. The HoC explained that it did hold the requested information, but there 
were low numbers involved (fewer than 5) which would make it possible 
for the data subject(s) to be identified.  
19. The HoC explained that the information requested relates to the 
Property and Asset Strategy (PAS) Team (a subset of the Strategic 
Estates Team) and the Strategic Estates (SE) Team as a whole. There 
are approximately 800 employees within the SE Team and 80 in PAS. 
20. It explained that a motivated intruder would be able to identify the data 
subject(s) if the requested information was disclosed. The HoC explained 
that the motivated intruder could simply be an individual employed by it 
and work in either PAS or SE and have a greater knowledge of and 
access to information about the organisation and structure of both 
teams than a general member of the public.   

Reference:  IC-398269-P5K6 
 
 4 
21. This motivated intruder would then be able to research role changes and 
job title changes over the period in question and which would lead to the 
data subject(s) being identified. 
22. This information therefore falls within the definition of ‘personal data’ in 
section 3(2) of the DPA. 
23. The fact that information constitutes the personal data of an identifiable 
living individual does not automatically exclude it from disclosure under 
FOIA. The second element of the test is to determine whether disclosure 
would contravene any of the DP principles.  
24. The most relevant DP principle in this case is principle (a). 
Would disclosure contravene principle (a)? 
25. Article 5(1)(a) of the UK GDPR states that: 
“Personal data shall be processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent 
manner in relation to the data subject”. 
26. In the case of an FOIA request, the personal data is processed when it is 
disclosed in response to the request. This means that the information 
can only be disclosed if to do so would be lawful, fair and transparent.  
27. In order to be lawful, one of the lawful bases listed in Article 6(1) of the 
UK GDPR must apply to the processing. It must also be generally lawful. 
Lawful processing: Article 6(1)(f) of the UK GDPR 
28. The Commissioner considers that the lawful basis most applicable is 
basis 6(1)(f) which states: 
“processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests 
pursued by the controller or by a third party except where such interests 
are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of 
the data subject which require protection of personal data, in particular 
where the data subject is a child”1. 
 
 
1 Article 6(1) goes on to state that:- 
“Point (f) of the first subparagraph shall not apply to processing carried out by public 
authorities in the performance of their tasks”. 
 
However, section 40(8) FOIA (as amended by Schedule 19 Paragraph 58(8) DPA) provides  
that:- 
 

Reference:  IC-398269-P5K6 
 
 5 
29. In considering the application of Article 6(1)(f) of the UK GDPR in the 
context of a request for information under FOIA, it is necessary to 
consider the following three-part test:- 
i) Legitimate interest test: Whether a legitimate interest is being 
pursued in the request for information; 
ii) Necessity test: Whether disclosure of the information is 
necessary to meet the legitimate interest in question; 
iii) Balancing test: Whether the above interests override the 
legitimate interest(s) or fundamental rights and freedoms of the 
data subject. 
30. The Commissioner considers that the test of ‘necessity’ under stage (ii) 
must be met before the balancing test under stage (iii) is applied. 
Legitimate interests 
31. In considering any legitimate interest(s) in the disclosure of the 
requested information under FOIA, the Commissioner recognises that 
such interest(s) can include broad general principles of accountability 
and transparency for their own sakes, as well as case specific interests. 
32. Further, a wide range of interests may be legitimate interests. They can 
be the requester’s own interests or the interests of third parties, and 
commercial interests as well as wider societal benefits. They may be 
compelling or trivial, but trivial interests may be more easily overridden 
in the balancing test. 
33. The complainant explained that they had requested this information to 
understand whether the HoC’s policy on grade and pay increases has 
been followed consistently over the requested period, especially within 
the SE department.  
34. The complainant added that they had been informed that grade 
increases for roles can only occur after an external grade evaluation. 
They wanted the information to determine if this was the case.  
35. Disclosing the requested information would demonstrate fairness and 
consistency in how staff are treated.  
 
 
“In determining for the purposes of this section whether the lawfulness principle in Article 
5(1)(a) of the UK GDPR would be contravened by the disclosure of information, Article 6(1) 
of the UK GDPR (lawfulness) is to be read as if the second sub-paragraph (dis-applying the 
legitimate interests gateway in relation to public authorities) were omitted ”. 

Reference:  IC-398269-P5K6 
 
 6 
36. Having considered the above, the Commissioner is satisfied that there is 
a legitimate interest in the requested information.  
Is disclosure necessary? 
37. ‘Necessary’ means more than desirable but less than indispensable or 
absolute necessity. Accordingly, the test is one of reasonable necessity 
and involves consideration of alternative measures which may make 
disclosure of the requested information unnecessary. Disclosure under 
FOIA must therefore be the least intrusive means of achieving the 
legitimate aim in question. 
38. Due to the small numbers involved in this case, the Commissioner is 
satisfied that there are no less intrusive ways for the complainant’s 
legitimate interests to be met.  
Balance between legitimate interests and the data subject’s interests 
or fundamental rights and freedoms 
39. It is necessary to balance the legitimate interests in disclosure against 
the data subject’s interests or fundamental rights and freedoms. In 
doing so, it is necessary to consider the impact of disclosure. For 
example, if the data subject would not reasonably expect that the 
information would be disclosed to the public under FOIA in response to 
the request, or if such disclosure would cause unjustified harm, their 
interests or rights are likely to override legitimate interests in disclosure. 
40. In considering this balancing test, the Commissioner has taken into 
account the following factors: 
• the potential harm or distress that disclosure may cause;  
• whether the information is already in the public domain;  
• whether the information is already known to some individuals;  
• whether the individual expressed concern to the disclosure; and  
• the reasonable expectations of the individual. 
41. In the Commissioner’s view, a key issue is whether the individuals 
concerned have a reasonable expectation that their information will not 
be disclosed. These expectations can be shaped by factors such as an 
individual’s general expectation of privacy, whether the information 
relates to an employee in their professional role or to them as 
individuals, and the purpose for which they provided their personal data. 
42. It is also important to consider whether disclosure would be likely to 
result in unwarranted damage or distress to that individual. 

Reference:  IC-398269-P5K6 
 
 7 
43. The HoC advised that, if the requested information were disclosed, a 
motivated intruder would be able to identify the data subject(s) and 
possibly approach them. The HoC explained that this would likely lead to 
the data subject(s) feeling uncomfortable and distressed. 
44. The HoC explained that the data subject(s) have a reasonable 
expectation that details of their professional life (including details of 
their work duties, pay and performance) be kept confidential.  
45. The Commissioner recognises that disclosing the requested information 
would show that the HoC is operating in an open and transparent 
manner.  
46. Based on the above factors, the Commissioner has determined that 
there is insufficient legitimate interest to outweigh the data subjects’ 
fundamental rights and freedoms. The Commissioner therefore 
considers that there is no Article 6 basis for processing and so the 
disclosure of the information would not be lawful. 
47. Given the above conclusion that disclosure would be unlawful, the 
Commissioner considers that he does not need to go on to separately 
consider whether disclosure would be fair or transparent. 

Reference:  IC-398269-P5K6 
 
 8 
Right of appeal  
48. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:  
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights) 
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
49. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website.  
50. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.  
 
 
 
 
Michael Lea 
Group Manager 
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF
description: The complainant has requested information about role upgrades. The House of Commons (the HoC) relied on section 40(2) of FOIA (third party personal information) to withhold the information. The Commissioner’s decision is that the HoC has correctly relied on section 40(2) of FOIA to withhold the information. The Commissioner does not require further steps to be taken.

title: Sheffield City Council
text:
Reference: IC-455221-R5G5 
 
 1 
Environmental Information Regulations 2004 (EIR) 
Decision notice 
 
 
 
 
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: Sheffield City Council 
Address: Town Hall  
Pinstone Street  
Sheffield  
S1 2HH 
1. Request: 
The complainant requested the following information from Sheffield City 
Council on 14 September 2025: 
“I request disclosure of all information held by Sheffield City Council 
relating to the closure of Montague Street, Sheffield, from 1 January 2018 
to the present date.  
Specifically, I request: 
1. Original Works Closure All decision records, dates, approvals, risk 
assessments, safety audits, and internal communications relating to the 
original closure of Montague Street to facilitate park works. Records 
showing the planned duration of the closure and the intended reopening 
date once works were completed.  
2. Bollard Installation All documents authorising the placement of bollards 
or other physical obstructions after the contractor’s compound was 
removed. The exact date of installation, the cost of works, and the officer 
or councillor who authorised this action. The stated reason for bollards 
being installed rather than reopening the road.  

Reference: IC-455221-R5G5 
 
 2 
3. Post-Works Deliberations Any internal reports, committee papers, 
emails, or correspondence considering whether the road should remain 
closed after works were complete. Any discussion or evaluation of 
alternative measures (traffic calming, signage, restricted hours) and 
reasons they were rejected.  
4. TTRO Decision (2025) All records relating to the decision to make the 
Montague Street (Prohibition of Vehicles) Order 2025, including: Risk 
assessments, collision/casualty data, and evidence relied upon to justify 
the claim of “likelihood of danger to the public”. Officer recommendations 
and decision-maker sign-off. 2 Whether the TTRO decision was prompted 
or accelerated by my formal complaint of [insert date].  
5. Equality, Accessibility, and Cost Any equality impact assessments or 
accessibility assessments relating to the ongoing closure. Any budget 
allocations or expenditure records connected to bollards, signage, or 
enforcement for this road.  
6. Councillor Involvement and Lobbying All records of meetings, emails, or 
communications involving councillors or council officers discussing the 
long-term closure of Montague Street. Any declarations of interest made by 
councillors (including Councillor [redacted name]) in relation to this matter. 
Any correspondence between the council and external lobby groups or 
individuals seeking to keep Montague Street closed, including discussions 
prior to or during the original works closure.  
7. Records Retention If any of the above information is no longer held, 
please confirm: The reason it is no longer held (e.g., routine destruction 
under a records retention schedule). The date on which the information 
was destroyed or deleted (if known).  
Format and Exemptions I request that information be supplied 
electronically. If any information is withheld, please: Specify which 
exception under the EIR is relied upon. Explain why disclosure would be 
against the public interest. Provide as much of the information as can 
reasonably be disclosed (with redactions if necessary). ” 
2. Commissioner’s Decision:  
A public authority will breach regulation 5(2) of the EIR if it fails to respond 
to a request within 20 working days. Based on evidence available to the 
Commissioner, by the date of this notice the public authority has not issued 
a substantive response to this request. Therefore the Commissioner finds a 
breach of regulation 5(2). 

Reference: IC-455221-R5G5 
 
 3 
 
 
Information Commissioner 
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF  
3. Steps: 
The public authority must provide a substantive response to the request in 
accordance with its obligations under the EIR.   
The public authority must take this step within 30 calendar days of the 
date of this decision notice. Failure to comply may result in the 
Commissioner making written certification of this fact to the High Court 
pursuant to section 54 of FOIA and may be dealt with as a contempt of 
court. 
4. Right of Appeal: 
Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the First-
tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals process 
may be obtained from: 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights) 
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-chamber  
Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 (calendar) 
days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.
description:
The complainant has requested information from Sheffield City Council regarding information in relation to the closure of Montague Street, 
Sheffield. Sheffield City Council has not provided a substantive response to the complainant’s request. It is the Commissioner’s view that Sheffield City Council has beached regulations 5(2) of the Environmental Information Regulations (“the EIR”) in that it failed to provide a valid response to the request within the statutory time frame of 20 working days. The Commissioner requires Sheffield City Council to provide the complainant with a response to their request in accordance with its obligations under the EIR.

title: Department For Education (DFE)
text:
Reference:  IC-400967-N7L2 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: Department for Education 
Address: Sanctuary Buildings 
 Great Smith Street 
                                     London SW1P 3PT 
 
Decision 
1. The complainant requested the costs incurred to date in the planning 
and execution of an alternative student finance (ASF) system, along 
with the projected costs to the taxpayer, including projected costs 
arising from defaulted and unpaid student loans. The Department for 
Education (DfE) disclosed the costs incurred to date but withheld the 
projected cost information under section 35(1)(a) of FOIA (formulation 
or development of government policy). 
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that DfE was entitled to withhold the 
information under section 35(1)(a) of FOIA.  
3. The Commissioner does not require further steps. 
Request and response 
4. On 18 March 2025, the complainant made the following request to DfE 
for information under FOIA: 
“Please provide the costs incurred so far in the planning and execution 
of an alternative student finance system and the projected costs to the 
taxpayer of such a system. These costs to include the projected costs of 
defaulted and unpaid student loans due to the length of loan exceeding 
the point where it is no longer payable.” 
5. On 24 April 2025, DfE responded to the request. With regard to the 
costs incurred so far, it said that excluding staff costs, and costs exempt 
under section 21 (information accessible by other means) in relation to 

Reference:  IC-400967-N7L2 
 2 
information published about spend on contracts, it holds information 
showing £360,670 spent on the ASF proposal since 2016. With regard to 
the projected costs to the taxpayer of the ASF system, DfE confirmed 
that it held some information within scope of the request but withheld it 
under section 35(1)(a) of FOIA. 
6. On 25 April 2025, the complainant wrote to DfE expressing their 
dissatisfaction with its response to both parts of the request. 
7. DfE completed an internal review. With regard to the costs incurred so 
far, after reviewing the information withheld under section 21, it 
recognised that the actual spend incurred differs from that in the 
publicly available contract values. DfE disclosed the total spend on the 
ASF proposal as £543,411.60 (excluding staff costs) to provide an 
accurate and complete figure. With regard to the projected costs to the 
taxpayer, it upheld its decision to withhold the information on 
application of section 35(1)(a) of FOIA. 
Scope of the case 
8. On 6 July 2025, the complainant contacted the Information 
Commissioner to complain about DfE’s application of section 35(1)(a) of 
FOIA to withhold information. 
9. The Commissioner has considered whether DfE is entitled to withhold 
information about the projected costs to the taxpayer for the ASF 
system under section 35(1)(a). 
Reasons for decision 
10. Section 35 of FOIA states:  
“(1) Information held by a government department or by the National 
Assembly for Wales is exempt information if it relates to –  
(a) The formulation or development of government policy” 
11.  The Commissioner understands these terms to broadly refer to the 
design of new policy, and the process of reviewing or improving existing 
policy. 
12.  The Commissioner’s guidance on section 35 explains that there is no 
standard form of government policy. Policy may be made in a number of 
different ways and take a variety of forms. Government policy does not 
have to be discussed in Cabinet and agreed by ministers. Policies can be 
formulated and developed within a single government department and 

Reference:  IC-400967-N7L2 
 3 
approved by the relevant ministers. The key point is that policymaking 
can take place in a variety of ways and there is no uniform process. 
13.  However, the Commissioner considers that the following factors will be 
key indicators of the formulation or development of government policy: 
• The final decision will be made either by the Cabinet or the relevant 
ministers; and 
• The government intends to achieve a particular outcome or change 
in the real world; and 
• The consequences of the decision will be wide ranging. 
14.  Section 35 of FOIA is class-based which means that departments do not 
need to consider the sensitivity of the information in order to engage the 
exemption. This is not a prejudice-based exemption, and the public 
authority does not have to demonstrate evidence of the likelihood of 
prejudice. The withheld information simply has to fall within the class of 
information described, in this case being the formulation or development 
of government policy. Classes can be interpreted broadly and will catch 
a wide range of information. 
15.  DfE explained that the government is committed to introducing policy to 
allow the introduction of a student finance product with an alternative to 
interest payments, as there are some people who cannot engage with 
interest bearing loans for reasons of faith or conscience, particularly 
Muslims whose faith prohibits engagement with interest. The ASF 
system will be certified by an independent Islamic Finance Supervisory 
Board and will operate on the Islamic finance principle of Takaful, 
offering a group based mutual support model. 
16.  DfE explained that the policy to which the information relates is the 
planning and execution of the ASF system, policy which is currently 
under development. Whilst developing this policy, DfE has: 
• consulted on the proposed ASF system model; 
 
• carried out research to check potential students’ views on 
alternative finance; 
 
• introduced primary legislation to allow an additional model of 
student finance that does not carry interest; 
 
• worked with Islamic finance experts to ensure that the ASF system 
introduced is compliant with Islamic finance principles; 
 

Reference:  IC-400967-N7L2 
 4 
• carried out an initial phase of work with the Student Loans Company 
(SLC) to understand delivery timescales and potential delivery 
solutions; and 
 
• appointed the Islamic Finance Council UK as secretariat to carry out 
the certification of the ASF product as compatible with Islamic 
finance principles. 
 
17. DfE said the government still needs to produce secondary legislation to 
set out key details of the ASF system, including the amount of finance 
students will be entitled to and how their contributions to the Takaful 
fund will be calculated. The ASF system must operate alongside the 
existing student loans system, and the SLC is currently working with the 
DfE on the policy design and delivery planning. As this secondary 
legislation is still being formulated, significant policy work remains 
ongoing. The ASF system will not be available until after the Lifelong 
Learning Entitlement is introduced, now expected from January 2027. 
18. The Commissioner notes that the request seeks projected costs to the 
taxpayer arising from the proposed ASF system, and that DfE has said 
this includes “modelling” on defaulted and unpaid loans.   
19. The Commissioner has reviewed the withheld information provided by 
DfE and notes that it shows projected costs of delivering and 
implementing the ASF system. He also notes that at the time of the 
request, the ASF policy including the structure of the ASF system 
through which it will operate, remained under active development. He 
also notes that DfE was, and still is, assessing feasibility, affordability, 
and value for money of the ASF system, and that the necessary 
secondary legislation is still being developed.  
20. Section 35(1)(a) requires only a significant link between the withheld 
information and the policy making process. At the time of the request, 
the information was actively being used to shape the ASF policy and 
inform its eventual design. The Commissioner is therefore satisfied that 
it relates to the development of government policy, and that section 
35(1)(a) is engaged. 
The Public Interest Test 
21. Section 35(1)(a) is a qualified exemption and is therefore subject to the 
public interest test. The Commissioner has considered the context of the 
information in order to determine whether the public interest in 
maintaining the exemption outweighs the public interest in favour of 
disclosure. 
 
 

Reference:  IC-400967-N7L2 
 5 
The public interest arguments in favour of disclosure 
22. DfE recognises a general public interest in disclosure of the projected 
cost information, as openness about information that informs 
government decisions helps the public understand how those decisions 
are made. This can lead to more meaningful public engagement in policy 
development. 
23. DfE recognises that disclosure promotes openness and transparency 
across government, particularly where it concerns the consideration of 
evidence around the development and fundamental changes to key 
government policies. 
24. DfE recognises that greater visibility of the process and delivery of the 
policy may lead to greater accountability, improve the quality of public 
debate, and improve public trust. 
25. DfE recognises the public interest in the delivery of the ASF system, 
including to prospective students, and that it also relates to ‘topical 
news’ surrounding public spending on student finance and the higher 
education sector. 
26. The complainant likewise recognises the public interest in open debate 
about the merits of an ASF system, especially in the current climate. 
They say that both past and projected spending are legitimate matters 
for public scrutiny, as the public has a right to understand the costs of a 
proposed policy and to hold ministers accountable for their decisions. 
27. The complainant said that no students are currently using the ASF 
system, and therefore releasing projected costs would not mislead them 
or the public, as they are presented as estimates, and that it is 
inappropriate to assume that readers would not understand this. Future 
students would not be confused by disclosure as they rely on trusted 
sources such as the SLC, teachers and carers. 
Arguments in favour of maintaining the exemption 
28. DfE explained that the withheld information relates to a long-standing 
DfE policy commitment, and that the development of the ASF policy is a 
high-profile policy area that is still ‘live’. 
29. DfE argues that policy development requires good government which 
depends on decisions informed by complete, robust, and uninhibited 
advice, and a protected “safe space” where ministers and officials can 
explore early ideas, financial projections, modelling, and advice without 
fear that unapproved or evolving positions will enter the public domain.  
30. DfE explained that discussions on the policy design are still ongoing, 
including how potential changes may affect the projected 

Reference:  IC-400967-N7L2 
 6 
implementation costs. The budget and associated costs for the ASF 
system have not yet been finalised. Any changes to these figures may 
require adjustments to the policy, as delivery outcomes depend on the 
level of funding available. Ministers have not yet formally approved all 
elements of the policy or its delivery approach, and these may be 
subject to change until full ministerial sign-off is secured. 
31. DfE argues that disclosing the withheld information would discourage 
and “dilute” the free, frank and candid nature of discussion and 
exchanges needed to explore policy options, risk confusion by exposing 
provisional positions before they are finalised, affect the ability to 
consider potential cost implications, and ultimately harm the quality and 
effectiveness of the policy. This would negatively influence and weaken 
policy development, and undermine effective government, which is not 
in the public interest.  
32. DfE also argues that disclosing the withheld information, may cause 
premature public scrutiny, and inhibit the open discussion needed 
between ministers and officials, and place pressure on them to commit 
to existing options or to change them following unwarranted and 
untimely public pressure, based on projected costings and modelling 
that are being considered as part of a currently incomplete policy. This 
would limit the exploration of certain options, prejudice eventual policy 
design decisions, and ultimately affect the quality, value for money, and 
outcome of the final policy. It is essential that it has the space to review, 
amend, and discuss any such changes, so as to accurately reflect the 
development path of this policy as set by the department.  
33. DFE also argues that disclosing information relating to ongoing policy 
development would likely harm relations between it and key partners 
(including SLC), preventing honest conversations and impacting service 
delivery in future.  
Balance of the public interest 
34. In considering the balance of the public interest, the Commissioner has 
considered all relevant factors, including the complainant’s arguments in 
favour of disclosure and DfE’s need for a protected space in which to 
formulate the ASF policy.  
35. The Commissioner recognises the public interest in transparency around 
the potential cost of new government policies, particularly those 
involving significant public expenditure. Disclosure could assist public 
understanding of the likely impact of the ASF system and contribute to a 
more informed debate about the value for money of proposed reforms to 
student finance.  
36. The Commissioner acknowledges the complainant’s view that the public 
would not be misled by “projected costs” but this does not address the 

Reference:  IC-400967-N7L2 
 7 
core purpose of section 35(1)(a): that policymaking relies on ministers 
and officials being able to test assumptions, challenge draft costings, 
explore alternative options and revise early modelling without the risk of 
premature external scrutiny. Even where the public is capable of 
interpreting estimates responsibly, disclosure at this stage would expose 
incomplete and still evolving financial projections which ministers and 
officials had not yet finalised, finished testing, or placed in their full 
policy context. 
37. The Commissioner similarly acknowledges the complainant’s view that 
accountability requires openness over projected spending. He also notes 
that at the time of the request, the ASF policy was, and still is, being 
developed. Key parameters including eligibility, contribution 
methodology, legislative underpinnings, and delivery costs were still 
under development. The projected costs were part of the working 
material used to assess feasibility and affordability. The Commissioner 
therefore accepts that releasing the withheld information before 
decisions are taken would undermine the ability of DfE officials to revise, 
challenge, and refine modelling freely, and risks distorting internal 
discussions in response to external commentary. 
38. The Commissioner acknowledges the complainant’s view that 
withholding the information denies the public a legitimate opportunity to 
scrutinise spending assumptions. He also notes that the public interest 
in scrutiny is significantly stronger once a policy is settled. At earlier 
stages, the risk of impairing the quality of policymaking is greater. In 
this case, disclosure would reveal figures which are subject to revision, 
and dependent on assumptions still being tested. Releasing the 
information would be likely to generate misunderstanding and 
unnecessary external pressure regardless of caveats, because it would 
be separated from the evolving internal context that gives it meaning. 
39. The Commissioner notes that DfE says disclosing the withheld 
information would risk undermining DfE’s its relations with SLC, with 
whom it is working through the underpinning design and delivery details 
of the ASF system.  
40. The Commissioner’s section 35 guidance stresses the need for a 
protected safe space for open and frank engagement when collaborating 
during live policy work, warning that premature disclosure can chill 
candour and damage trust with delivery partners. Given SLC’s 
continuing role, any loss of trust could directly impede DfE’s ability to 
obtain the necessary frank input during ongoing delivery planning. 
41. Having carefully considered all factors, the Commissioner concludes that 
the public interest in maintaining a safe space for the ongoing 
formulation of the ASF policy outweighs the arguments in favour of 
disclosure. The Commissioner therefore finds that the public interest 

Reference:  IC-400967-N7L2 
 8 
favours maintaining the exemption under section 35(1)(a), and DfE was 
entitled to withhold the projected cost information. 
Right of appeal  
42. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:
