First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights)   
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
43. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website.  
44. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.  
 
 
 
Pamela Clements 
Group Manager 
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF
description: The complainant requested the costs incurred to date in the planning and execution of an alternative student finance (ASF) system, along with the projected costs to the taxpayer, including projected costs arising from defaulted and unpaid student loans. The Department for Education (DfE) disclosed the costs incurred to date but withheld the projected cost information under section 35(1)(a) of FOIA (formulation or development of government policy).The Commissioner’s decision is that DfE was entitled to withhold the information under section 35(1)(a) of FOIA. The Commissioner does not require further steps.

title: Cabinet Office
text:
Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: Cabinet Office 
Address: 70 Whitehall 
London SW1A 2AS 
  
 
 
Decision (including any steps ordered) 
1. The complainant has requested from the Cabinet Office (CO) records 
relating to a communication between Donald Trump and Keir Starmer 
regarding an incident at Trump Turnberry golf course in Scotland. CO 
refused to provide this information citing section 27 (international 
relations) as its basis for doing so. It upheld this following an internal 
review. 
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that CO is entitled to rely on provisions 
of section 27 as its basis for refusing to provide the requested 
information. 
3. The Commissioner does not require further steps 
Request and response 
4. On 31 March 2025 the complainant requested information of the 
following description: 
“Background 
In March 2025, President Trump posted the following on Truth Social. 
‘I was just informed by Prime Minister Starmer of the United Kingdom, 
that they caught the terrorists who attacked the beautiful Turnberry, in 
Scotland. They did serious damage, and will hopefully be treated 

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 2 
harshly. The three people who did this are in prison. You cannot let 
things like this attack happen, and I greatly appreciate the work of 
Prime Minister Starmer, and UK Law Enforcement.’ 
Request 
Please provide 
1. All records of the contact between the Prime Minister and President 
Trump, including audio-visual records, briefings, readouts, minutes, 
emails, text messages, hand written notes, post it notes, recording or 
preparing for the above referenced communication between the two 
people or their representatives.” 
5. On 30 April 2025, CO responded. It refused to provide the requested 
information. It cited the following exemptions as its basis for doing so:  
- section 27(1)(a), (c) and (d). 
6. It explained that the discussion included a number of live areas relating 
to diplomacy and global affairs and provided this link in respect of it: 
https://www.gov.uk/government/news/pm-call-with-president-trump-
of-the-united-states-30-march-2025 . 
7. The complainant requested an internal review on 1 May 2025. They 
chased a response on 24 June 2025 and 6 July 2025. 
8. CO provided an internal review on 22 July 2025. It upheld its original 
position. 
Scope of the case 
9. The complainant contacted the Commissioner on 8 July 2025 to 
complain about the way their request for information had been handled.  
10. The Commissioner considers that the scope of his investigation is to 
decide whether CO can rely on section 27(1)(a), (b)1, (c) and (d) as its 
basis for withholding the requested information. The Commissioner 
notes that CO only needs to rely on one of these provisions to withhold 
the requested information as long as all the information engages that 
provision and the balance of the public interest favours its maintenance. 
The Commissioner’s role in considering application of exemptions is 
 
 
1 See paragraph 15. 

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 3 
limited to considering the circumstances at the time the request should 
have been responded to in accordance with statutory timeframes for 
compliance. That is, at the 20 working days limit. 
Reasons for decision 
Background 
11. On 8 March 2025, the Turnberry golf course (which is owned by US 
President Donald Trump) was vandalised. 
https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cr52q7l6drdo 
12. The group Palestine Action claimed responsibility for the vandalism. At 
the time of this incident (and at the time of the request) Palestine Action 
was not a proscribed group. It was subsequently proscribed on 5 July 
2025.2 Palestine Action sought to challenge this decision3 and on 13 
February 2026 the High Court found the decision to proscribe Palestine 
Action was unlawful4. 
Section 27 – international relations  
13. CO initially relied on sections 27(1)(a), (c) and (d) of FOIA to withhold 
the information it acknowledged it held.  
14. These state that: “Information is exempt information if its disclosure 
under this Act would, or would be likely to, prejudice-  
(a) relations between the United Kingdom and any other State…  
(c) the interests of the United Kingdom abroad, or  
(d) the promotion or protection by the United Kingdom of its interests 
abroad”  
15. During the course of the Commissioner’s investigation, it also introduced 
reliance on section 27(1)(b). This states that disclosure would or would 
be likely to prejudice relations between the United Kingdom and any 
international organisation or international court.  
 
 
2 https://www.gov.uk/government/news/three-groups-to-be-proscribed; 
https://www.protectuk.police.uk/news-views/palestine-action-proscribed  
3 https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cqlerg1n67no  
4 https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c3wleezq73no  

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 4 
CO’s position 
16. In its letter of internal review to the complainant it explained why 
section 27(1) was engaged. CO said: 
“The content of the discussion in question involves sensitive diplomatic 
communications between the UK and the United States, a key 
international partner. Releasing such information could reasonably be 
expected to undermine the mutual trust and confidence that underpins 
the bilateral relationship, potentially damaging the UK’s ability to engage 
in open and effective dialogue with the U.S. Government and other 
international partners in the future”. 
17. In its response to the Commissioner, CO described the positive personal 
relationship between Prime Minister and the President and cited a press 
conference on 28 July 2025 as an example of this. It provided a 
YouTube link and drew the Commissioner’s attention to 48:27 minutes 
into that conference.5  Although that conference postdated the request 
by some months, the Commissioner understands CO to also be applying 
their description of the relationship to the time of the request in this 
case. 
18. It stressed the importance of being able to guarantee confidentiality in 
such exchanges and noted the fact that the conversation in question 
took place shortly before the request. 
19. It said:  
“A likely outcome of the disclosure of the information is that the 
President would take into account in the future the possibility that 
details of his calls with the Prime Minister would end up in the public 
domain. As a result, he would be less inclined to engage in calls with the 
Prime Minister, or would be more guarded in what he said on such calls. 
It should be apparent that such an outcome would undermine the 
relationship between the UK and the United States, making the conduct 
of diplomatic business much more difficult. If the relationship between 
the UK and the United States were impaired, it would also be likely to 
make it more difficult for the UK to safeguard its overseas interests.” 
20. Regarding section 27(1)(b), it said: 
“We also take the view that the disclosure of the information would 
undermine the relationship that the UK has with other countries on the 
basis that those leaders could reasonably assume that the calls that 
 
 
5 https://youtu.be/K4gZ0qFJ8gw (46:27) 

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 5 
they also have with the Prime Minister may be vulnerable to disclosure. 
The consequence of this is that they too would be likely to avoid having 
calls with the Prime Minister or be overly circumspect in what they say, 
knowing that there is a risk that the contents of the call could be put 
into the public domain. This would have a deleterious effect on UK 
diplomacy generally and make much harder the diplomatic role that the 
Prime Minister plays.  
We further note that this effect would also be likely to be felt in respect 
of calls that are conducted between the Prime Minister and leaders of 
international organisations. It would therefore follow that the 
information ought also be exempt from disclosure under section 
27(1)(b) of the Act on the grounds that its disclosure would be likely to 
prejudice the relations between the UK and any international 
organisation”. 
21. Following further engagement with the Commissioner, CO also made 
additional representations with specific reference to the withheld 
information. These representations and the content of the engagement 
are addressed in a Confidential Annex to this Notice. 
The complainant’s position  
22. The complainant raised a number of points in favour of disclosure. These 
addressed both the likelihood of prejudice and, where the exemptions at 
section 27 are engaged, the public interest in favour of disclosure. 
23. The complainant’s argument was that there had been undue external 
influence involved in the July 2025 decision to proscribe Palestine 
Action. While their request predated that decision, the complainant 
made further representations while waiting for CO to conduct its internal 
review. On 6 July 2025, they drew attention to a recent High Court 
judgment (Ammori -v- Secretary of State for the Home Department 
[2025] EWHC 1708 (Admin)) which at paragraph 52b noted that “The 
Home Secretary was advised as early as 13 March 2025 that the 
statutory tests for proscription were met”.6 
24. The Commissioner would note that where such advice was given to the 
Home Secretary, it predated the conversation between the President 
and the Prime Minister referred to in the request. That said, the 
complainant also drew attention to publicly available information about 
an intervention by one of the President’s sons on 13 March 2025. Eric 
Trump met with the First Minister of Scotland as a representative of the 
 
 
6 https://www.judiciary.uk/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/ammori-judgment-revised.pdf 

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 6 
Trump Organisation. This was disclosed on 10 April 2025 by the Scottish 
Government in response to an FOIA request.7 
25. The complainant also said: 
“President Trump intentionally published the specific content of the call, 
at least in part, and as a result there can be no detriment or prejudice to 
international relations with the USA, arising by the PMO releasing its full 
record of the call, because it no longer has the quality of confidential 
information. This is a specific and exceptional case so no precedent 
would be established that might cause detriment in future cases.” 
The Commissioner’s position  
26. In order for a prejudice based exemption, such as section 27, to be 
engaged the Commissioner believes that three criteria must be met:  
• Firstly, the actual harm which the public authority alleges would, or 
would be likely, to occur if the withheld information was disclosed has to 
relate to the applicable interests within the relevant exemption.  
• Secondly, the public authority must be able to demonstrate that some 
causal relationship exists between the potential disclosure of the 
information being withheld and the prejudice which the exemption is 
designed to protect. Furthermore, the resultant prejudice which is 
alleged must be real, actual or of substance.  
• Thirdly, it is necessary to establish whether the level of likelihood of 
prejudice being relied upon by the public authority is met – i.e., 
disclosure would be likely to result in prejudice or disclosure would 
result in prejudice. If the likelihood of prejudice occurring is one that is 
only hypothetical or remote the exemption will not be engaged.  
27. Furthermore, the Commissioner has been guided by the comments of 
the Information Tribunal which suggested that in the context of section 
27(1), prejudice can be real and of substance ‘if it makes relations more 
difficult or calls for a particular damage limitation response to contain or 
limit damage which would not have otherwise have been necessary.’ 8 
28. With regard to the first criterion of the three limb test described above, 
the Commissioner accepts that the potential prejudice described by CO 
 
 
7 https://www.whatdotheyknow.com/request/scottish_first_minister_meeting  
8 
https://informationrights.decisions.tribunals.gov.uk/DBFiles/Decision/i205/Campaign%20Ag
ainst%20the%20Arms%20Trade;%20EA.2007.0040%20.pdf (paragraph 81) 

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 7 
clearly relates to the interests which the exemptions contained at 
sections 27(1)(a), (b), (c) and (d) are designed to protect.  
29. With regard to the second and third criteria, the Commissioner 
recognises a causal link between disclosure of the information in this 
case and the prejudicial outcomes described in the exemptions. He 
notes the complainant’s observation regarding that which has already 
been put into the public domain by President Trump. He does not 
consider this reduces the likelihood of a prejudicial outcome for 
international relations to the point where it no longer applies. 
30. In reaching this conclusion, the Commissioner is mindful of the age of 
the information. Conversations at this level are confidential. A country’s 
leader speaking with the UK Prime Minister in this way would not expect 
the details to be published immediately. It is reasonable to conclude that 
releasing this information could harm the UK’s relations with other 
states as well as with the United States regardless of any unilateral 
commentary from one party to those conversations. 
31. Having considered the withheld information itself and the 
representations made, the Commissioner has concluded that sections 
27(1)(a), (b), (c) and (d) are engaged. As such, he is satisfied that 
disclosure of the withheld information would be likely to prejudice the 
relations / interests covered by each of the provisions. 
Public interest test  
32. Sections 27(1)(a), (b), (c) and (d) are qualified exemptions and 
therefore subject to the public interest test set out in section 2(2)(b) of 
FOIA. The Commissioner has considered whether in all the 
circumstances of the case the public interest in maintaining the 
exemptions cited by CO outweighs the public interest in disclosing the 
information.  
CO’s arguments 
33. Referring to the prejudicial outcomes set out above, CO asserted a 
strong public interest in maintaining the exemptions. There would be, it 
argued, “no benefit to the conduct of diplomacy by the UK if the Prime 
Minister were unable to carry out calls with his international 
counterparts without such calls being restricted in some way”. 

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 8 
34. It argued that the press release which it published shortly after the call 
met the public interest in transparency in this case. 9 
35. The Commissioner asked further more detailed questions about the 
balance of the public interest with specific reference to the withheld 
information. These are considered in the Confidential Annex to this 
Notice. 
The complainant’s position 
36. The complainant said: 
“There is … a pressing and vital public interest in the disclosure of PMO 
[Prime Minister’s Office] records of the contents of this particular 
conversation as it w[ould] assure the public that there was no 
misconduct in public office by the Prime Minister, and no pressure was 
placed on the UK government by the US President, to treat the arrested 
suspects of the Trump Turnberry protest "harshly" and as "terrorists" at 
a time before the highly controversial proscription of Palestine Action by 
the UK government, had been decided and during sensitive US - UK 
trade negotiations where the Prime Minister was seeking to win the 
favour of President Trump to gain reductions on tariffs on UK exports to 
the USA.” 
37. Referring to the disclosures they had drawn to the Commissioner’s 
attention, the complainant said “none of the official Scottish or UK 
announcements make any mention of the discussions of the Trump 
Turnberry action, yet internal records confirm these took place. 
It follows that UK and Scottish government officials have failed in their 
public duty to be fully proactively transparent about important 
discussions with a foreign power that appear very likely to have 
influenced an unprecedented decision to criminalise civil liberties and the 
fundamental freedom to protest in the UK. 
This fact clearly increases an already overwhelming public interest in 
disclosure of the records of this phone call, that far outweighs any 
possible detriment to international relations that might possibly arise”. 
The Commissioner’s conclusion 
38. The Commissioner recognises the very strong public interest in 
maintaining the confidentiality of conversations between world leaders, 
particularly where those conversations are recent. There is a significant 
 
 
9 https://www.gov.uk/government/news/pm-call-with-president-trump-of-the-united-states-
30-march-2025  

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 9 
public interest in ensuring the effectiveness of the UK’s relations with 
other states so that it is able to protect and promote its interests 
39. That said, the complainant makes serious allegations that there was 
undue influence brought to bear on the decision to proscribe Palestine 
Action.  
40. However, in relation to the timeline suggested by the complainant, the 
Commissioner is mindful of the following matters which are in the public 
domain.  
• The vandalism at Turnberry was on 8 March.  
• The court case at Note 6 indicates that the Home Office had 
concluded that the tests for proscribing Palestine Action had been 
met on 13 March.  
• The President’s son met with the First Minister of Scotland on 13 
March.  
• The President says that his conversation of 30 March with the 
Prime Minister included discussion of the vandalism at Turnberry.  
41. It is highly unlikely that the Home Office’s conclusion was influenced by 
a conversation between the US President’s son and the First Minister of 
Scotland—who has no authority over proscription decisions—on the 
same day, nor that this was subsequently expedited as a result of that 
meeting. The Commissioner notes that, prior to the vandalism at 
Turnberry, there had been more serious incidents during Palestine 
Action protests, including an alleged sledgehammer attack on a security 
guard.10 
42. Further commentary about the balance of public interest is in a 
Confidential Annex to this Notice. 
Conclusion 
43. Taking the submissions of both parties into account and having read the 
withheld information, the Commissioner is satisfied that the balance of 
the public interest favours the maintenance of each of the exemption 
provisions at section 27(1)(a), (b), (c) and (d).  The Commissioner 
therefore accepts the CO’s bases for withholding the requested 
information. 
 
 
10 Palestine Action activists 'caused £1m of damage in raid on Israeli defence firm HQ' | The 
Standard 

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 10 
Other matters 
44. The Commissioner notes that there was a delay in responding to the 
complainant’s request for an internal review in respect of his request.  
45. Part VI of the section 45 Code of Practice makes it desirable practice for 
a public authority to have a procedure in place for dealing with 
complaints about its handling of requests for information and that the 
procedure should encourage a prompt determination of the complaint.  
46. As the Commissioner has made clear in his guidance, he considers that 
these internal reviews should be completed as promptly as possible. 
While no explicit timescale is laid down by the FOIA, the Commissioner 
considers that a reasonable time for completing an internal review is 20 
working days from the date of the request for review. In exceptional 
circumstances it may be reasonable to take longer but in no case should 
the time taken exceed 40 working days.11  
47. In this case, the request for an internal review was made on 1 May 2025 
and the response was issued on 22 July 2025. The Commissioner notes 
that in this case, the time taken to respond was 59 working days.   
48. The Commissioner therefore finds that this delay did not accord with his 
guidance.  
 
 
 
11 https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/foi/guide-to-managing-an-foi-request/complaints-
internal-reviews/  

Reference: IC-401801-S8Z8  
 11 
Right of appeal  
49. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:  
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights)   
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
50. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website.  
51. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.  
 
 
 
 
Alexander Ganotis 
Group Manager 
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF

title: London Borough Of Barking and Dagenham Council
text:
Reference: IC-473136-M8J4 
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
 
Date:  13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: London Borough of Barking and Dagenham 
Address: Barking Town Hall 
1 Town Square 
Barking 
IG11 7LU 
 
 
 
1. Request: 
The complainant requested the following information London Borough of 
Barking and Dagenham on 17 November 2025: 
 
“I hereby request several pieces of information pertaining to the 
questions below and answers to be received within 20 working days 
upon this request being received.  
 
1. How much has LBBD spent on fly-tipping costs in the last 10-
years. To confirm, this includes resourcing to clear up fly-tipping 
across the borough, campaign materials to discourage fly-tipping 
across the borough, additional cost of overtime of staff etc. I am 
requesting this be broken down by year. 
  
2. How many people/individuals have the LBBD caught fly-tipping in 
the last 10-years?  
 
3. How much money has the LBBD spent on installing CCTV at fly-
tipping hotspots over the last 10 years. I'm requesting this 
breakdown per year.  
 
 

Reference: IC-473136-M8J4 
 
 2 
4.  How much has the LBBD spent on 3rd party recycling and 
rubbish/refuge collection agencies over the last 10-years. I'm 
requesting this breakdown per year.”  
 
2. Commissioner’s Decision:  
A public authority will breach section 10 of FOIA if it fails to respond to a 
request within 20 working days. Based on evidence available to the 
Commissioner, by the date of this notice the public authority has not issued 
a substantive response to this request. Therefore the Commissioner finds a 
breach of section 10. 
3. Steps: 
The public authority must provide a substantive response to the request in 
accordance with its obligations under FOIA.   
The public authority must take this step within 30 calendar days of the 
date of this decision notice. Failure to comply may result in the 
Commissioner making written certification of this fact to the High Court 
pursuant to section 54 of FOIA and may be dealt with as a contempt of 
court.

title: National Police Chiefs' Council (NPCC)
text:
Reference:  IC-405813-K6S5 
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026  
  
Public Authority: National Police Chiefs’ Council 
Address: PO Box 481 
Fareham 
Hampshire 
PO14 9FS 
  
 
 
Decision (including any steps ordered) 
1. The complainant requested from National Police Chiefs’ Council (NPCC) 
information regarding cross-force access. NPCC provided information 
relating to parts 5a and 5b of the request and confirmed that it does not 
hold any further information.  
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that on the balance of probabilities, 
NPCC does not hold any further information falling within scope of the 
request. Therefore, the Commissioner does not require NPCC to take 
any steps because of this decision. 
Request and response 
3. On 17 February 2025 the complainant wrote to NPCC and requested 
information in the following terms: 
“This request concerns the cross-force access to biometric, 
misconduct-related, and other special category data via the Police 
National Computer (PNC) by the Cambridgeshire-led Professional 
Standards Department (PSD) under the tri-force policing collaboration 
with Bedfordshire and Hertfordshire Constabularies.  
 
 

Reference:  IC-405813-K6S5 
 
 2 
The subject matter raises grave and unresolved questions around data 
protection compliance, internal governance, access control, and 
oversight in relation to the PNC and associated systems. I am therefore 
seeking the following information, covering the period from January 
2018 to the present, unless otherwise specified. 
 
1. Legal Basis and Data Sharing Frameworks  
 
a. Please provide copies of any Data Sharing Agreements, Joint 
Controllership Agreements, or Memoranda of Understanding 
governing access to PNC or PNC-derived data by PSD personnel in 
one force (e.g. Cambridgeshire) when investigating officers or 
incidents originating in another (e.g. Hertfordshire).  
 
b. Please confirm whether any internal legal reviews, DPIAs, or 
information sharing impact assessments have been conducted in 
relation to this cross-force use of PNC data for internal misconduct 
investigations.  
 
c. If no such agreements or assessments exist, please confirm this 
explicitly.  
 
2. Oversight and Accountability Mechanisms  
 
a. Please describe the governance structures and national oversight 
mechanisms in place to regulate and audit PSD access to PNC data 
under cross-force arrangements. 
 
b. Has ACRO, the NPCC, or any Home Office body issued guidance or 
enforcement notices concerning the management of tri-force or 
collaborative PSD data access to PNC?  
 
c. Are there any published or internal audit findings or reviews relating 
to misuse or overreach of access to PNC data by Professional 
Standards Departments since 2018?  
 
3. Technical Access Controls and Logging  
 
a. Please explain what role-based access control mechanisms are 
enforced on PNC or affiliated systems to restrict PSD personnel from 
one force accessing sensitive data originating from another.  
 
b. What audit trail systems or automatic alerts are in place to log and 
flag access by cross-force personnel to biometric or internal 
misconduct records? 
 

Reference:  IC-405813-K6S5 
 
 3 
c. Are any systematic compliance checks or user access reviews 
conducted across tri-force PSD access to PNC or internal force 
databases? 
 
4. Transparency and Data Subject Rights  
 
a. Is it national or force policy to notify data subjects when their 
biometric or misconduct-related data is accessed by officers in a 
different force?  
 
b. Please provide copies of any guidance issued on data subject 
transparency, notification, or redress when cross-force access 
occurs.  
 
c. If no such policy exists, please confirm this clearly.  
 
5. Controllership, Liability and Reporting  
 
a. Who is considered the data controller under UK GDPR and the Data 
Protection Act 2018 when PNC linked data is accessed by PSD 
investigators from another force?  
 
b. Please provide any NPCC or ACRO guidance or policies regarding 
liability, redress, or internal reporting obligations in the event of 
inappropriate or excessive cross-force access.  
 
c. Has ACRO received or logged any complaints, breach reports, or 
internal referrals relating to the use of PNC data in tri-force or 
cross-force PSD investigations since January 2018?” 
 
4. On 10 July 2025 NPCC responded. It stated it does not hold recorded 
information captured by parts 1b, 1c, 2a to 2c, 3b, 3c, 4a to 4c and 5c. 
NPCC said it does hold information to parts 1a, 3a, 5a and 5b and it 
provided this information to the complainant.  
5. The complainant subsequently asked for an internal review. 
6. On 31 July 2025 NPCC provided its review response and maintained its 
original position.  
Reasons for decision 
7. This reasoning covers why, on the balance of probabilities, NPCC does 
not hold any further information that falls within scope of the request. 

Reference:  IC-405813-K6S5 
 
 4 
Section 1 – information held/not held  
8. Section 1 of FOIA states that any person making a request for 
information to a public authority is entitled to be informed in writing by 
the public authority whether it holds information within the scope of the 
request, and if so, to have that information communicated to him.  
9. The public authority is not obliged to create or acquire information to 
satisfy a request.  
10. The Commissioner’s role when determining whether a public authority 
has complied with section 1(1) of FOIA, is limited to whether it is more 
likely than not that the public authority has provided all the recorded 
information it holds. The Commissioner is not required to challenge the 
accuracy or adequacy of this information. This is because the terms of 
FOIA only relate to the provision of information as it is recorded, 
regardless of its accuracy or validity.  
11. Where there is some dispute between the amount of information 
identified by a public authority and the amount of information a 
complainant believes may be held, the Commissioner is led by several 
First-tier Tribunal decisions. He must decide whether on the civil 
standard of the balance of probabilities, the public authority holds any 
information which falls within scope of the request (or was held at the 
time of the request). 
The complainant’s position 
12. The complainant raised concerns that NPCC’s response did not include 
information which she believes falls within scope of the request.  
13. The complainant considered the information NPCC provided, (parts 5a 
and 5b of the request) was only a generic PNC Joint Controllership 
Agreement. She said it does not address “the specific matter of inter-
force PSD access for misconduct and employment-related inquiries. No 
function-specific DPIA, ISA, or legal assessment was disclosed.” 
14. The complainant referred to the response from NPCC stating it does not 
hold information regarding: Access reviews; Internal audits; 
Enforcement actions or complaints concerning PSD misuse of the PNC. 
She said that these omissions suggest either a systemic failure of 
governance or a failure to comply with FOIA in good faith.  
15. The complainant emphasised that the request concerned access by 
Professional Standards Departments to PNC data across force 
boundaries under the tri-force collaboration between Cambridgeshire, 
Bedfordshire, and Hertfordshire. She said the request was about legal 
basis, data protection governance, technical controls, and oversight.  

Reference:  IC-405813-K6S5 
 
 5 
16. The complainant believes the information NPCC disclosed, contained 
nothing of relevance, and listed information which she said should have 
been included: local DPIAs; data sharing agreements tailored to the tri-
force context; logging mechanisms; access reviews; redress framework; 
confirmation of controllership. The complainant argued that in the 
internal review response, NPCC had failed to address what she considers 
critical legal and procedural issues. 
17. The complainant argued the response from NPCC stating no information 
was held regarding records, systems and policies. The complainant said 
this implies that PSD officers can access another force’s misconduct data 
without jurisdictional limits, oversight, or monitoring. An absence of 
controls that is, in the complainant’s view, unlawful. The complainant’s 
position is she strongly believes NPCC holds further information, and 
that it had not conducted thorough searches relating to the request.    
NPCC’s position 
18. NPCC explained to the Commissioner the structure of NPCC in which the 
National Police Freedom of Information and Data Protection Unit 
(NPFDU) respond to all FOI requests. It said there are currently over 
300 ‘working groups’ within NPCC, and that this highlights the structure 
and vast nature of NPCC and the research which is conducted to 
determine what information is held for each request.  
19. NPCC further explained that while it maintains some records centrally, 
due to its structure and legal framework, it does not operate a single 
central records system. Each chief officer is employed and hosted by 
their respective police force, so records are held in line with that force’s 
systems and policies. As a result, although NPCC can search the records 
it keeps centrally, additional information may also be held within host 
forces, systems to which NPCC does not have centralised access. 
Therefore, NPCC cannot conduct a search of a single central database 
because no such database exists.  
20. NPCC informed the Commissioner of the searches it conducted to check 
for information within scope of the request. It said it engaged with the 
various policing leads that it could reasonably identify. This included: 
ACRO – Criminals Records Office; Counter Corruption; Data Protection; 
PNC lead and PSD lead. NPCC said all the areas (excluding PNC) 
confirmed no information is held. 
21. NPCC described the searches of relevant paper/electronic records it had 
carried out and staff consultations. It reiterated the details above 
(paragraph 20) of the policing leads which the NPCC engaged with.  

Reference:  IC-405813-K6S5 
 
 6 
22. NPCC supplied the Commissioner with an annex which shows evidence 
of the searches conducted. Regarding any recorded information ever 
held relevant to the scope of the request but deleted/destroyed, NPCC 
said “no – not that is known by me”.  
23. Regarding its formal records management policy, NPCC said it would 
adhere to ACPO Retention Records and Disposal (RR&D) policy. This also 
applies to any statutory requirements upon the NPCC to retain the 
requested information.  
24. NPCC was asked if the information is electronic data which has been 
deleted, might copies have been made and held in other locations. NPCC 
replied “not known/unable to comment on” and referred the 
Commissioner to is explanation of NPCC’s structure (paragraph 19 
above) which it considers “highlights the unique structure”.  
25. NPCC was also asked whether there is a business purpose for which the 
requested information should be held and if so, what is this purpose. 
NPCC said information was held and provided and added “it is believed 
no further information is held in scope of the applicant’s requests.” 
The Commissioner’s position  
26. It is not the Commissioner’s position to determine what information 
ought to be held by NPCC in this matter, but rather what is held, and 
whether appropriate searches have been conducted.  
27. Regarding the complainant’s view that the information provided by NPCC 
to parts of the request were incomplete, the Commissioner emphasised 
paragraph 10 above. He is not required to assess whether that 
information is accurate or adequate, as FOIA concerns only the 
disclosure of recorded information, not its quality or validity. 
28. The Commissioner notes that the complainant believes NPCC holds 
further information relating to the request, and thinks it is deliberately 
withholding it from disclosure. He is also aware of the complainant’s 
view that NPCC should conduct “proper searches and issue a full, lawful 
response.” While the complainant refuses to accept NPCC’s response, 
the Commissioner has found no evidence which would indicate NPCC 
holds further information. He also sees no reason to doubt NPCC’s 
explanation.  
29. The Commissioner considered NPCC’s actions to check whether it holds 
further information relating to the request. He recognises that NPCC had 
engaged with the various policing leads regarding searching information. 
The Commissioner accepts its explanation on the structure of NPCC and 
responding to FOI requests. The Commissioner is satisfied NPCC carried 
out appropriate searches for the information.  

Reference:  IC-405813-K6S5 
 
 7 
30. Whilst the complainant may believe the information disclosed to him is 
unacceptable, and that further information must be held, at the point 
the Commissioner investigated the matter, he concludes the NPCC did 
not hold any further information in scope of the request. 
Conclusion 
31. Based on the evidence provided to him, the Commissioner is satisfied 
that, on the balance of probabilities, NPCC does not hold any further 
information relating to the request. NPCC therefore complied with the 
requirements of section 1(1) of FOIA in this case.  
 

Reference:  IC-405813-K6S5 
 
 8 
Right of appeal  
32. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:  
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights) 
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
33. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website.  
34. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.  
 
 
 
 
Joanna Marshall  
Group Manager  
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF

title: Newport City Council
text:
Reference: IC-386724-S2S5  
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: Newport City Council 
Address: Civil Centre  
Godfrey Road 
Newport 
NP20 4UR 
 
Decision (including any steps ordered) 
1. The complainant requested a report relating to a research project on 
homelessness and LGBTQ+ people in Gwent. Newport City Council (the 
council) provided a redacted copy of the report, citing section 41 of FOIA 
(information provided in confidence) to withhold the redacted 
information. 
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that the council was entitled to rely on 
section 41(1) to withhold the redacted information.  
3. The Commissioner does not require the council to take any steps as a 
result of this decision notice. 
Request and response 
4. On 15 March 2025, the complainant made the following request for 
information to the council: 
“I am looking for a copy of the research done by Ty Pawb, that 
was commissioned by Newport City Council, alongside other 
Gwent local authorities. I believe it's titled, Homelessness and 
LGBTQ+ People in Gwent Research. I would also like to know if 
Newport City Council has implemented any changes regarding 
LGBTQ+ homelessness as a result of this report.” 

Reference: IC-386724-S2S5  
 
 2 
5. The council responded on 9 April 2025, refusing to provide the report 
under section 41 of FOIA on the basis that it was provided in confidence. 
In response to the question about whether the council had implemented 
any changes as a result of the report, it stated:  
“Due to a number of operational challenges including the Covid 
pandemic; the Welsh Government’s ‘Every One In’ approach and 
the consequential large rise in the number of people housed in 
temporary accommodation; the changes to homelessness 
legislation regarding people sleeping rough, and the substantial 
rise in the number of people presenting with complex needs as 
well as a restructure in all housing departments/teams, it has not 
been possible to prioritise many of the changes outlined in the 
report.”  
6. The complainant wrote to the council on 9 April 2025, requesting that it 
carry out an internal review of its decision.  
7. The council provided the outcome of its internal review on 14 May 2025, 
in which it provided a copy of the requested report but with some 
information redacted. It maintained that section 41 applied to the 
redacted information. 
Scope of the case 
8. The complainant contacted the Commissioner on 14 May 2025 to 
complain about the way their request for information had been handled.  
9. The complainant disagrees with the way the council has applied section 
41 in this case and believes the report has been excessively redacted. In 
addition, the complainant understands that the report has been shown 
to Wales Online as part of its Wales Online Awards and therefore doesn’t 
believe that the document has the expected quality of confidentiality 
under section 41. 
10. The Commissioner considers the scope of this case is to determine 
whether the council is entitled to rely on section 41 of FOIA to withhold 
the redacted information. 
Reasons for decision 
The information at issue in this case 
11. In this case, the requested information is a report produced by the 
housing charity Tai Pawb into homelessness and LGBTQ+ people in 

Reference: IC-386724-S2S5  
 
 3 
Gwent. The information that has been redacted from the report consists 
of information provided to Tai Pawb by organisations that it engaged 
with as part of its research. 
Section 41 – information provided in confidence 
12. Section 41 aims to protect information given to a public authority in 
confidence. It allows them to refuse to disclose information in response 
to an FOI request if they can show that: 
• they have received the information from someone else. This can 
also be another public authority or an organisation; and 
• disclosing it would expose the public authority to a breach of 
confidence claim which is likely to succeed. 
13. If the public authority received the information via a third party, it may 
still owe a duty of confidence to the original confider. 
Was the information obtained from another person? 
14. The council has explained that although the report was commissioned by 
the council, it was undertaken and authored by Tai Pawb in collaboration 
with Cardiff Metropolitan University. The substantive content therefore 
originated with external organisations and was provided to the council 
as part of the commissioned work. The information is not the council’s 
own creation and was supplied to it by third parties. 
15. The Commissioner also notes that the redacted information was itself 
provided to Tai Pawb by other organisations as part of Tai Pawb’s 
research. 
16. The Commissioner is therefore satisfied that the redacted information 
was obtained from another person. 
Would disclosure constitute an actionable breach of confidence? 
17. To decide if disclosure would result in a breach of confidence, the 
Commissioner follows the three-part test set out by Judge Megarry in 
the High Court decision in Coco v A.N. Clark (Engineers) LtD [1969] RPC 
41 [para. 47]. This is often called the “Coco test.” 
18. There are three elements to the test. These are: 
• The information must have the “necessary quality of confidence”. 
• The information was imparted in circumstances importing an 
obligation of confidence. 

Reference: IC-386724-S2S5  
 
 4 
• There is an unauthorised use of the information. 
Does the information have the necessary quality of confidence? 
19. The Commissioner considers that to have the necessary quality of 
confidence, information must not be trivial or trite, and it must be 
generally inaccessible to the public and not public knowledge. 
20. The council argued that the redacted information is not publicly available 
and contains sensitive information about very small LGBTQ+ support 
projects and grassroots organisations. It includes details of their staffing 
arrangements and operational vulnerabilities. The council stated that 
this information is highly significant to the organisations that provided it 
and therefore requires protection. 
21. In response to the complainant’s understanding that the report has been 
shown to Wales Online as part of its Wales Online Awards, the council 
has explained that the report itself was not shared with Wales Online. 
The council stated that the nomination for the award was submitted 
through an application form and was based on the project rather than 
the finalised report, which had not been completed at the time the 
award was announced. 
22. The Commissioner is therefore satisfied that the withheld information 
has the necessary quality of confidence because it is not trivial or trite 
and it is not generally accessible to the public. 
Was the information imparted in circumstances importing an 
obligation of confidence? 
23. The Commissioner considers that an obligation of confidence can be 
expressed explicitly or implicitly. Whether there is an implied obligation 
of confidence will depend upon the nature of the information itself or the 
relationship between the parties involved, or both. 
24. The council has explained that the redacted information was gathered 
through confidential research interviews and engagement activities. 
Participants and organisations were given both explicit and implicit 
assurances that any identifiable or sensitive material would not be 
disclosed publicly. It argues that the relationship between Tai Pawb, the 
contributing organisations, and the council was founded on a clear 
expectation of confidentiality. 
25. The Commissioner is therefore satisfied that the requested information 
would have been imparted to Tai Pawb in circumstances importing an 
obligation of confidence, and that this duty of confidence to the original 
confiders of the information passed to the council when it was provided 
with the report by Tai Pawb. 

Reference: IC-386724-S2S5  
 
 5 
Would disclosure be an unauthorised use of the information? 
26. The council contends that disclosure could put small, low-capacity 
community services and their staff at risk by exposing them to 
harassment, targeting by hostile actors, or online abuse. It argued that 
releasing the information could also undermine their ability to operate 
safely and divert scarce resources away from frontline work. 
27. The Commissioner agrees that disclosure would amount to an 
unauthorised use of the information, to the detriment of the confiders. 
In particular, disclosure could expose small, low-capacity services and 
their staff to harassment or online abuse and could undermine their safe 
operation. 
Is there a public interest defence for disclosure?  
28. Section 41 is an absolute exemption and so there is no requirement for 
an application of the conventional public interest test. However, 
disclosure of confidential information where there is an overriding public 
interest is a defence to an action for breach of confidentiality. The 
Commissioner is therefore required to consider whether the council 
could successfully rely on such a public interest defence to an action for 
breach of confidence in this case.  
29. The Commissioner recognises that the courts have taken the view that 
the grounds for breaching confidentiality must be valid and very strong 
since the duty of confidence is not one which should be overridden 
lightly.  
30. Whilst much will depend on the facts and circumstances of each case, a 
public authority should weigh up the public interest in disclosure of the 
information requested against both the wider public interest in 
preserving the principle of confidentiality and the impact that disclosure 
of the information would have on the interests of the confider.  
31. As the decisions taken by courts have shown, very significant public 
interest factors must be present in order to override the strong public 
interest in maintaining confidentiality, such as where the information 
concerns misconduct, illegality, or gross immorality. 
32. The council considered the general public interest in openness and 
transparency but found this was outweighed by the risks of disclosing 
sensitive information about small LGBTQ+ support organisations and 
their service users.  
33. The council has argued that the redacted report already provides the 
main findings and recommendations, which meets the public interest in 
understanding how homelessness affects LGBTQ+ people. It stated that 

Reference: IC-386724-S2S5  
 
 6 
disclosure of the withheld information would not add materially to public 
understanding but would increase the risk of harm to the safety, 
wellbeing and operational capacity of the organisations involved.  
34. On balance, the council concluded that maintaining confidentiality was in 
the public interest and that disclosure would amount to a breach of 
confidence. 
35. The Commissioner has considered whether a public interest defence 
would justify disclosure of the withheld information and thereby defeat 
an action for breach of confidence. A public interest defence requires 
compelling factors such as revealing wrongdoing, illegality, serious 
maladministration, or an immediate risk to public safety. 
36. Based on the information provided to the Commissioner, there is no 
indication that disclosure would reveal such factors. The material at 
issue concerns sensitive information about a small number of individuals 
and community organisations. The Commissioner considers that 
disclosure would create a real and foreseeable risk of harm, while adding 
little of substance to the information already available through the 
disclosed findings and recommendations. 
37. The Commissioner therefore finds that the balance of the public interest 
favours maintaining the obligation of confidence. As the Commissioner 
considers that there is no public interest defence that would defeat an 
action for breach of confidence in this case, he is satisfied that 
disclosure would be actionable, and that the redacted information can 
therefore be withheld under section 41(1) of FOIA. 

Reference: IC-386724-S2S5  
 
 7 
Right of appeal  
38. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:  
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights)   
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
39. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website.  
40. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.  
 
 
 
 
Michelle Armitt 
Senior Case Officer 
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF

Newport City Council (the council) provided a redacted copy of the report, citing section 41 of FOIA (information provided in confidence) to withhold the redacted information. The Commissioner’s decision is that the council was entitled to rely on section 41(1) to withhold the redacted information. The Commissioner does not require the council to take any steps as a result of this decision notice.

title: Department For Education (DFE)
text:
Reference: IC-405867-Z5Z8 
 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
    
Date: 13 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: Department for Education 
Address: Sanctuary Buildings 
Great Smith Street 
London  
SW1P 3PT 
  
Decision (including any steps ordered) 
1. The complainant has requested information about a referral to the 
Teaching Regulation Agency (‘TRA’). The Department for Education (“the 
public authority”) refused to confirm or deny the requested information 
was held, citing section 40(5B) (personal information) of FOIA. 
2. The Commissioner’s decision is that to confirm or deny the information 
is held would be a breach of the data protection principles and so the 
public authority was correct to cite section 40(5). 
3. The Commissioner does not require further steps.  
Request and response 
4. On 20 June 2025, the complainant wrote to the public authority and 
made a request for information. The request asked for cost related 
information relating to a referral made to the TRA.  
5. The public authority responded on 23 June 2025. It refused to confirm 
or deny that it held the requested information, citing section 40(5B) of 
FOIA.  
6. The complainant requested an internal review on 24 June 2025.  
7. The public authority responded on 16 July 2025. It upheld its previous 
position.  

Reference: IC-405867-Z5Z8 
 
 2 
Scope of the case 
8. The complainant contacted the Commissioner on 16 July 2025 to 
complain about the way their request for information had been handled.  
9. The Commissioner considers that the scope of his investigation is to 
consider whether the public authority was correct to refuse to confirm or 
deny holding the requested information.  
Reasons for decision 
Section 40 – personal information 
10. Section 1(1)(a) of FOIA outlines a public authority’s duty to confirm 
whether it holds the information being requested. This is known as the 
‘duty to confirm or deny.’ 
11. However, section 40(5B) removes that obligation if confirming or 
denying information is held would, in itself, disclose the personal data of 
a third party and contravene one of the data protection principles.  
12. In this case, the complainant has requested information relating to a 
referral to the TRA. If the public authority confirms it holds the 
requested information, it essentially confirms such a referral was made.  
13. This is personal data about whomever the referral relates but only if 
they can be identified by the request itself.  
14. There is no individual named in the request, it doesn’t identify anyone 
directly. However, the complainant has a well-publicised grievance with 
a teacher, details of which are in the public domain. 
15. For this reason, and looking at the specific wording of the request, the 
Commissioner is satisfied that this teacher (‘the data subject’) can be 
indirectly identified from the request and the requested information, if 
held, would relate to them.  
16. The TRA is an executive agency of the DfE and regulates teachers. Part 
of its role is to investigate alleged misconduct and carry out misconduct 
hearings.  
17. Only if a complaint is upheld, the Secretary of State will bar the teacher 
from teaching. All parties involved will be informed, the decision is 

Reference: IC-405867-Z5Z8 
 
 3 
published online1 and the teacher’s name is added to the list of 
prohibited teachers held by the TRA.  
18. This is the only scenario in which a teacher’s referral to the TRA would 
be made publicly available.  
19. Returning to the request, if the public authority confirms the requested 
information is held, it confirms the TRA has considered a complaint 
about the data subject. If it denies the requested information is held, it 
confirms the TRA hasn’t considered such a complaint. Either way, this is 
the data subject’s personal data.  
20. So, personal data would be disclosed be either confirmation or denial 
but the Commissioner must go onto consider whether to do so would 
contravene any of the data protection principles.  
21. The most relevant principle is Article 5(1)(a) of the UK General Data 
Protection Regulation (‘UK GDPR’) which states: 
“Personal data shall be processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent 
manner in relation to the data subject.” 
22. Personal data is processed when it’s disclosed in response to a FOIA 
request. In order to be lawful under Article 5(1)(a), the lawful basis 
under Article 6(1)(f) of the UK GDPR must apply to the processing. It 
must also be generally lawful.  
23. In order to determine whether disclosure, or in this case, confirmation 
or denial, would be lawful, the Commissioner considers three ‘tests’: the 
legitimate interest test, the necessity test, and the balancing test. 
24. The complainant has, as previously explained, a grievance about the 
data subject. It’s a legitimate interest for them to have. There’s also a 
general legitimate interest in accountability and transparency, which 
extends to how the public authority and the TRA deal with complaints. 
25. The Commissioner has next considered whether disclosure, through 
confirmation or denial, would be necessary to address the legitimate 
interests that have been identified. If there are alternate, less-intrusive, 
means by which the legitimate interest(s) can be met, disclosure may 
not be necessary. 
26. The public authority doesn’t believe that there are any alternate means 
which would place the requested information, or the confirmation or 
 
 
1 panel outcome misconduct - Search - GOV.UK 

Reference: IC-405867-Z5Z8 
 
 4 
denial, into the public domain in this instance. So disclosure or denial is 
necessary. 
27. The Commissioner has considered what the request is trying to achieve. 
It’s not just whether a referral has been made but the financial 
breakdown associated with any such referral. Even if a referral to the 
TRA results in an outcome, as described in paragraph 17, the associated 
financial breakdown wouldn’t be made public.  
28. Also, if any referral doesn’t result in an outcome, the only way to know 
whether the TRA had considered the matter would be by disclosure to 
the world at large or, by being the individual who made such a referral.  
29. The Commissioner agrees with the public authority, confirmation or 
denial is necessary in this instance and so he’s gone onto consider the 
balancing test.  
30. When balancing the legitimate interests the request represents, a 
variety of factors will be taken into account: the potential harm or 
distress to the data subject, whether the information is already in the 
public domain, whether the information is already known to some 
individuals, whether the individual expressed concern to the disclosure, 
and their reasonable expectations. 
31. To reiterate, details of the grievance the complainant has about the data 
subject is already in the public domain. However, this doesn’t include 
whether the data subject has been subject to any TRA referral. To 
disclose this information to the world at large would undoubtedly cause 
distress to the data subject, who wouldn’t reasonably expect a regulator 
to confirm whether a complaint had been raised against them outside of 
its established processes.  
32. The Commissioner has decided that, whilst confirmation or denial is 
necessary to meet the legitimate interest in this request, these 
legitimate interests don’t outweigh the rights and freedoms of the data 
subject. There is no lawful basis for disclosure, or confirmation or denial, 
and so the public authority was correct to refuse to confirm or deny it’s 
held under section 40(5).  

Reference: IC-405867-Z5Z8 
 
 5 
Right of appeal  
33. Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals 
process may be obtained from:  
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights)   
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963 
Fax: 0870 739 5836 
Email: grc@justice.gov.uk  
Website: www.justice.gov.uk/tribunals/general-regulatory-
chamber  
 
34. If you wish to appeal against a decision notice, you can obtain 
information on how to appeal along with the relevant forms from the 
Information Tribunal website.  
35. Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 
(calendar) days of the date on which this decision notice is sent.  
 
 
 
Alice Gradwell 
Senior Case Officer 
Information Commissioner’s Office  
Wycliffe House  
Water Lane  
Wilmslow  
Cheshire  
SK9 5AF
description: The complainant has requested information about a referral to the Teaching Regulation Agency (‘TRA’). The Department for Education (“the public authority”) refused to confirm or deny the requested information was held, citing section 40(5B) (personal information) of FOIA. The Commissioner’s decision is that to confirm or deny the information is held would be a breach of the data protection principles and so the public authority was correct to cite section 40(5). The Commissioner does not require further steps.

title: Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust
text:
Reference: IC-474974-J0B9 
 1 
Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) 
Decision notice 
 
 
Date: 12 March 2026 
  
Public Authority: Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust 
Address: St Thomas’ Hospital 
Westminster Bridge Road 
London 
SE1 7EH 
 
1. Request: 
The complainant requested the following information from Guy's and St 
Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust on 18 December 2025: 
 
“I am writing to request the following information under the Freedom of 
Information Act 2000. This request relates solely to non-personal, 
organisational, system-level and policy information. 
Unless otherwise stated, the information requested applies to the period 1 
January 2023 to 31 December 2024. Where a more specific period is 
identified, please apply that period only. 
 
CALL-HANDLING SYSTEMS 
1. What telephone or call-handling systems are used by: 
a) the Patient Advice and Liaison Service (PALS), 
b) the Early Pregnancy and Acute Gynaecology Unit (EPAGU), 
c) the Emergency Department (ED), and 
d) the Trust’s Complaints Department. 
2. Whether these systems automatically record any of the following call 
metadata: 
a) incoming call date and time, 
b) outgoing call date and time, 
c) call duration, 

Reference: IC-474974-J0B9 
 2 
d) missed or unanswered calls, 
e) voicemail messages left, 
f) call attempt status. 
3. Whether outbound call attempts made by staff within PALS, EPAGU, 
ED or the Complaints Department are automatically logged by the 
system, or require manual recording. 
 
CALL LOGS AND RECORD-KEEPING 
4. Whether staff are required or encouraged to manually record 
telephone contacts with patients, and if so: 
a) where such records are held, and 
b) whether such records form part of the official record. 
5. Whether the absence of a call log or record is treated as confirmation 
that a call did not occur. 
6. The retention period for: 
a) call metadata, and 
b) manual call logs or notes. 
INCOMING CALL MANAGEMENT 
7. How incoming calls are managed by PALS, EPAGU, ED and the 
Complaints Department. 
8. Whether incoming calls: 
a) are queued, 
b) diverted to voicemail, 
c) receive recorded messages, or 
d) are terminated if unanswered. 
9. Who answers incoming calls and whether triage or prioritisation is 
used. 
10. Procedures followed when calls cannot be answered, including    
redirection. 
11. Whether unanswered, abandoned calls or peak volumes are 
monitored and reviewed. 
USE OF CALL RECORDS IN COMPLAINTS 
12. Whether call records are relied upon in PALS enquiries or formal 
complaints. 

Reference: IC-474974-J0B9 
 3 
13. Any guidance or SOPs governing logging and reliance on call records. 
EPAGU TELEPHONE SERVICE AVAILABILITY 
Relevant period: 1 October 2023 to 31 January 2024 
14. Whether EPAGU operated as voicemail-only during this period. 
15. Details of any voicemail-only periods. 
16. Whether authorisation was required and documented. 
17. Reasons recorded for voicemail-only operation. 
18. Alternative access arrangements communicated. 
19. Whether such periods were logged or reviewed. 
20. Policies governing suspension or modification of EPAGU telephone 
access. 
VERIFICATION, AUDIT AND RELIABILITY 
21. Whether call systems generate audit trails. 
22. Whether call data can be altered and whether changes are logged. 
23. Safeguards ensuring accuracy and integrity of call logs. 
24. Known limitations or reliability issues. 
25. Instances of missing or incomplete call logs. 
VERIFICATION IN COMPLAINT INVESTIGATIONS 
26. Steps taken to verify call records. 
27. Requirement to consider system limitations. 
28. Guidance or training on evaluating call evidence. 
CONTEMPORANEOUS RECORDS 
29. Whether call-backs are recorded in other systems. 
30. Whether investigators cross-check records. 
 

Reference: IC-474974-J0B9 
 4 
TELEPHONE BILLING DATA 
31. Whether itemised billing data is held. 
32. What information billing data contains. 
33. How billing data is used. 
34. Whether billing data is used for cross-checking. 
35. Retention period for billing data. 
36. Policies governing billing data use.” 
2. Commissioner’s Decision:  
A public authority will breach section 10 of FOIA if it fails to respond to a 
request within 20 working days. Based on evidence available to the 
Commissioner, by the date of this notice the public authority has not issued 
a substantive response to this request. Therefore the Commissioner finds a 
breach of section 10. 
3. Steps: 
The public authority must provide a substantive response to the request in 
accordance with its obligations under FOIA.   
The public authority must take this step within 30 calendar days of the 
date of this decision notice. Failure to comply may result in the 
Commissioner making written certification of this fact to the High Court 
pursuant to section 54 of FOIA and may be dealt with as a contempt of 
court. 
4. Right of Appeal: 
Either party has the right to appeal against this decision notice to the First-
tier Tribunal (Information Rights). Information about the appeals process 
may be obtained from: 
First-tier Tribunal (Information Rights) 
General Regulatory Chamber 
PO Box 11230 
Leicester 
LE1 8FQ 
 
Tel: 0203 936 8963

Any Notice of Appeal should be served on the Tribunal within 28 (calendar)

description: The complainant requested information from Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust. The Commissioner’s decision is that the public authority failed to respond to the request within 20 working days and has therefore breached section 10 of FOIA. The public authority must provide a substantive response to the request in accordance with its obligations under FOIA.
